verses.explainer: But if the spot is unchanged and has not spread, it is only a scar from the boil. The priest pronounces them clean.

verses.text: “Or when the body has a burn from fire on its skin, and the raw flesh of the burn becomes a bright spot, reddish-white, or white,
verses.explainer: When someone has a burn on their skin and a reddish-white or white spot appears in the raw flesh of the burn.

verses.text: then the priest shall examine it; and behold, if the hair in the bright spot has turned white, and its appearance is deeper than the skin; it is leprosy. It has broken out in the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean. It is the plague of leprosy.
verses.explainer: The priest examines it. If the hair has turned white in the spot and it appears deeper than the skin, a disease has broken out in the burn. The priest pronounces the person unclean.

verses.text: But if the priest examines it, and behold, there is no white hair in the bright spot, and it isn’t lower than the skin, but is faded; then the priest shall isolate him seven days.
verses.explainer: But if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in the spot and it is not deeper than the skin and has faded, the priest isolates the person for seven days.

verses.text: The priest shall examine him on the seventh day. If it has spread in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean. It is the plague of leprosy.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day the priest examines them. If it is spreading, the priest pronounces them unclean.

verses.text: If the bright spot stays in its place, and hasn’t spread in the skin, but is faded, it is the swelling from the burn, and the priest shall pronounce him clean; for it is the scar from the burn.
verses.explainer: If the spot is unchanged and has not spread in the skin but has faded, it is a swelling from the burn. The priest pronounces them clean because it is only a scar from the burn.

verses.text: “When a man or woman has a plague on the head or on the beard,
verses.explainer: If a man or woman has a sore on the head or chin.

verses.text: then the priest shall examine the plague; and behold, if its appearance is deeper than the skin, and the hair in it is yellow and thin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is an itch, it is leprosy of the head or of the beard.
verses.explainer: The priest examines the sore. If it appears deeper than the skin and the hair in it is yellow and thin, the priest pronounces them unclean. It is a defiling disease of the head or chin.

verses.text: If the priest examines the plague of itching, and behold, its appearance isn’t deeper than the skin, and there is no black hair in it, then the priest shall isolate the person infected with itching seven days.
verses.explainer: But if the priest examines the disease and it does not appear deeper than the skin and there is no black hair in it, the priest isolates the person for seven days.

verses.text: On the seventh day the priest shall examine the plague; and behold, if the itch hasn’t spread, and there is no yellow hair in it, and the appearance of the itch isn’t deeper than the skin,
verses.explainer: On the seventh day the priest examines it. If it has not spread and there is no yellow hair and it does not appear deeper than the skin.

verses.text: then he shall be shaved, but he shall not shave the itch; and the priest shall shut him up who has the itch seven more days.
verses.explainer: The person must shave except for the affected area. The priest isolates them for another seven days.

verses.text: On the seventh day, the priest shall examine the itch; and behold, if the itch hasn’t spread in the skin, and its appearance isn’t deeper than the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him clean. He shall wash his clothes, and be clean.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day the priest examines it. If it has not spread and does not appear deeper, the priest pronounces them clean. They wash their clothes and are clean.

verses.text: But if the itch spreads in the skin after his cleansing,
verses.explainer: But if the disease spreads after they are pronounced clean.

verses.text: then the priest shall examine him; and behold, if the itch has spread in the skin, the priest shall not look for the yellow hair; he is unclean.
verses.explainer: The priest examines them. If it has spread, the priest does not need to look for yellow hair -- the person is unclean.

verses.text: But if in his eyes the itch is arrested, and black hair has grown in it; the itch is healed, he is clean. The priest shall pronounce him clean.
verses.explainer: But if in his judgment the disease is unchanged and black hair has grown in it, the disease is healed. The person is clean, and the priest pronounces them clean.

verses.text: “When a man or a woman has bright spots in the skin of the body, even white bright spots;
verses.explainer: When a man or woman has white spots on the skin.

verses.text: then the priest shall examine them; and behold, if the bright spots on the skin of their body are a dull white, it is a harmless rash, it has broken out in the skin; he is clean.
verses.explainer: The priest examines them. If the spots are dull white, it is a harmless rash. The person is clean.

verses.text: “If a man’s hair has fallen from his head, he is bald. He is clean.
verses.explainer: A man who loses his hair and is bald is clean. Baldness is not a disease.

verses.text: If his hair has fallen off from the front part of his head, he is forehead bald. He is clean.
verses.explainer: If he has lost his hair from the front of his scalp and has a bald forehead, he is clean.

verses.text: But if there is in the bald head, or the bald forehead, a reddish-white plague; it is leprosy breaking out in his bald head, or his bald forehead.
verses.explainer: But if he has a reddish-white sore on his bald head or forehead, it is a disease breaking out.

verses.text: Then the priest shall examine him; and, behold, if the rising of the plague is reddish-white in his bald head, or in his bald forehead, like the appearance of leprosy in the skin of the flesh,
verses.explainer: The priest examines it. If the swollen sore is reddish-white like a defiling disease on the skin.

verses.text: he is a leprous man. He is unclean. The priest shall surely pronounce him unclean. His plague is on his head.
verses.explainer: The man is diseased and unclean. The priest must pronounce him unclean because of the sore on his head.

verses.text: “The leper in whom the plague is shall wear torn clothes, and the hair of his head shall hang loose. He shall cover his upper lip, and shall cry, ‘Unclean! Unclean!’
verses.explainer: Anyone with a defiling skin disease must wear torn clothes, let their hair be unkempt, cover the lower part of their face, and cry out 'Unclean! Unclean!' The social consequences are devastating -- the person becomes a walking warning sign, forced to advertise their contamination.

thenVsNow.then: The public declaration of uncleanness was a quarantine measure that protected the community from contagion. But it also created profound social isolation -- the person was cut off from family, worship, and normal life.
thenVsNow.now: Modern medicine has replaced ritual quarantine with medical quarantine, but the social stigma of disease persists. The experience of isolation during infectious disease -- tragically illustrated in modern pandemics -- echoes the ancient reality that illness separates people from community.

verses.text: All the days in which the plague is in him he shall be unclean. He is unclean. He shall dwell alone. Outside of the camp shall be his dwelling.
verses.explainer: As long as they have the disease, they remain unclean. They must live alone outside the camp. Permanent exile from the community for the duration of the disease. This is the background for understanding why Jesus touching a leper was so radical.

verses.text: “The garment also that the plague of leprosy is in, whether it is a woolen garment, or a linen garment;
verses.explainer: If a garment is contaminated with mold -- any garment of wool or linen -- the contamination laws extend beyond human skin to clothing and fabrics.

verses.text: whether it is in warp, or woof; of linen, or of wool; whether in a skin, or in anything made of skin;
verses.explainer: Whether in the warp or woof of linen or wool, or in leather or anything made of leather.

verses.text: if the plague is greenish or reddish in the garment, or in the skin, or in the warp, or in the woof, or in anything made of skin; it is the plague of leprosy, and shall be shown to the priest.
verses.explainer: If the contamination in the garment or leather or woven or knitted material or leather article is greenish or reddish, it is a spreading mold and must be shown to the priest.

verses.text: The priest shall examine the plague, and isolate the plague seven days.
verses.explainer: The priest examines the mold and isolates the affected article for seven days.

verses.text: He shall examine the plague on the seventh day. If the plague has spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in the skin, whatever use the skin is used for, the plague is a destructive mildew. It is unclean.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day he examines it. If the mold has spread, it is a persistent, defiling mold. The article is unclean.

verses.text: He shall burn the garment, whether the warp or the woof, in wool or in linen, or anything of skin, in which the plague is: for it is a destructive mildew. It shall be burned in the fire.
verses.explainer: He must burn the garment, whether the warp or woof in wool or linen, or any leather article with the mold. It is a persistent mold and must be burned.

verses.text: “If the priest examines it, and behold, the plague hasn’t spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in anything of skin;
verses.explainer: But if the priest examines it and the mold has not spread in the garment.

verses.text: then the priest shall command that they wash the thing in which the plague is, and he shall isolate it seven more days.
verses.explainer: He orders the contaminated article to be washed and isolates it for another seven days.

verses.text: Then the priest shall examine it, after the plague is washed; and behold, if the plague hasn’t changed its color, and the plague hasn’t spread, it is unclean; you shall burn it in the fire. It is a mildewed spot, whether the bareness is inside or outside.
verses.explainer: After the article has been washed, the priest examines it. If the mold has not changed its appearance even though it has not spread, it is unclean. Burn it. Whether the mold is on the front or back, it must be destroyed.

verses.text: If the priest looks, and behold, the plague has faded after it is washed, then he shall tear it out of the garment, or out of the skin, or out of the warp, or out of the woof:
verses.explainer: If the priest examines it and the mold has faded after washing, he tears the affected part out of the garment or leather.

verses.text: and if it appears again in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in anything of skin, it is spreading. You shall burn with fire that in which the plague is.
verses.explainer: But if it reappears in the garment or leather, it is a spreading mold. Whatever has the mold must be burned.

verses.text: The garment, either the warp, or the woof, or whatever thing of skin it is, which you shall wash, if the plague has departed from them, then it shall be washed the second time, and it will be clean.”
verses.explainer: Any garment that has been washed and the mold has disappeared, it is to be washed again, and it will be clean.

verses.text: This is the law of the plague of mildew in a garment of wool or linen, either in the warp, or the woof, or in anything of skin, to pronounce it clean, or to pronounce it unclean.
verses.explainer: These are the regulations for defiling molds in woolen or linen clothing or woven or knitted material or any leather article, for pronouncing them clean or unclean. The comprehensive nature of these regulations demonstrates that holiness touches every aspect of material life.

explainer.summary: This chapter provides the purification ritual for healed skin diseases -- one of the most elaborate ceremonies in Leviticus. It involves two birds (one killed, one released alive), cedar wood, scarlet yarn, hyssop, and a seven-day process including shaving all body hair. The ritual symbolically transfers the person's uncleanness to the live bird, which carries it away. The chapter also addresses mold contamination in houses with similar inspection, quarantine, and purification procedures.
themes: The live bird carrying away impurity
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 completes the skin disease unit. Chapter 13 diagnosed the disease and excluded the person; chapter 14 restores them. The elaborate ceremony shows that restoration is costly and important -- being welcomed back into community is worth celebrating.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Cedar was associated with durability and incorrupt
wordStudies.meaning: Cedar was associated with durability and incorruptibility -- it resists rot and insects. In purification rituals, it likely symbolized permanence and health.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A bushy plant used for sprinkling in purification
wordStudies.meaning: A bushy plant used for sprinkling in purification rituals. Its fibrous branches effectively distributed liquid. Symbolically, it represented purification and was used at the Passover (Exodus 12:22) and in David's prayer for cleansing (Psalm 51:7).

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the regulations for the diseased person at the time of their ceremonial cleansing.

verses.text: “This shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing. He shall be brought to the priest,
verses.explainer: These are the regulations for anyone who has a defiling skin disease and who is being ceremonially cleansed when they are brought to the priest. The purified person does not self-certify -- the priest must confirm the healing.

verses.text: and the priest shall go out of the camp. The priest shall examine him, and behold, if the plague of leprosy is healed in the leper,
verses.explainer: The priest goes outside the camp and examines the person. If the disease has been healed, the priest goes to where the person is living -- outside the camp. The priest comes to the outcast, not the other way around.

verses.text: then the priest shall command them to take for him who is to be cleansed two living clean birds, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop.
verses.explainer: The priest orders two live clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop for the person being cleansed. Two birds: one will die, one will live. The dual role anticipates the Day of Atonement's two goats.

verses.text: The priest shall command them to kill one of the birds in an earthen vessel over running water.
verses.explainer: The priest orders that one of the birds be killed over fresh water in a clay pot. The bird's blood mixes with 'living water' -- fresh, flowing water, not stagnant.

verses.text: As for the living bird, he shall take it, and the cedar wood, and the scarlet, and the hyssop, and shall dip them and the living bird in the blood of the bird that was killed over the running water.
verses.explainer: He then takes the live bird together with the cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop, and dips them in the blood of the bird that was killed over the fresh water. The living bird is dipped in the blood of the dead bird -- absorbing the death symbolically.

verses.text: He shall sprinkle on him who is to be cleansed from the leprosy seven times, and shall pronounce him clean, and shall let the living bird go into the open field.
verses.explainer: He sprinkles the person being cleansed seven times and pronounces them clean. Then he releases the live bird in the open field. The bird flies away carrying the impurity with it -- a visible symbol of contamination being removed and sent far from the community.

thenVsNow.then: The released bird carrying away impurity was a powerful visual. The cleansed person watched their disease symbolically fly away, never to return. It made abstract purification concrete and visible.
thenVsNow.now: The image of sin or impurity being carried away resonates with the Christian understanding of Jesus 'bearing our sins' and the concept of forgiveness as removal of guilt -- 'as far as the east is from the west' (Psalm 103:12).

verses.text: “He who is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and bathe himself in water; and he shall be clean. After that he shall come into the camp, but shall dwell outside his tent seven days.
verses.explainer: The person being cleansed must wash their clothes, shave off all their hair, and bathe with water. Then they will be ceremonially clean. After this they may come into the camp but must stay outside their tent for seven days. The restoration is gradual: first back into the camp, but not yet back into their tent. Full reintegration takes time.

verses.text: It shall be on the seventh day, that he shall shave all his hair off his head and his beard and his eyebrows, even all his hair he shall shave off. He shall wash his clothes, and he shall bathe his body in water, then he shall be clean.
verses.explainer: On the seventh day they must shave off all their hair -- head, beard, eyebrows, all of it. They wash their clothes and bathe, and they are clean. Complete shaving removes any trace of the old, contaminated state. The person emerges with a completely new exterior.

verses.text: “On the eighth day he shall take two male lambs without defect, and one ewe lamb a year old without defect, and three tenths of an ephah of fine flour for a meal offering, mingled with oil, and one log of oil.
verses.explainer: On the eighth day they bring two male lambs and one ewe lamb, a year old and without blemish, along with fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, and one log of oil. The eighth-day offerings are substantial, reflecting the significance of the restoration.

verses.text: The priest who cleanses him shall set the man who is to be cleansed, and those things, before Yahweh, at the door of the Tent of Meeting.
verses.explainer: The priest who pronounces them clean presents both the person and their offerings before the Lord at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: “The priest shall take one of the male lambs, and offer him for a trespass offering, with the log of oil, and wave them for a wave offering before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The priest takes one of the male lambs and offers it as a guilt offering, along with the log of oil, and waves them before the Lord as a wave offering. A guilt offering is required because the disease has kept the person from fulfilling their obligations to God.

verses.text: He shall kill the male lamb in the place where they kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the place of the sanctuary; for as the sin offering is the priest’s, so is the trespass offering. It is most holy.
verses.explainer: He slaughters the lamb in the holy place where the sin offering and burnt offering are slaughtered. Like the sin offering, the guilt offering belongs to the priest. It is most holy.

verses.text: The priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering, and the priest shall put it on the tip of the right ear of him who is to be cleansed, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.
verses.explainer: The priest takes some of the blood of the guilt offering and puts it on the lobe of the right ear of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot. The same ear-thumb-toe application used in priestly ordination (chapter 8). The healed person is being reconsecrated -- their hearing, work, and walk rededicated to God.

verses.text: The priest shall take some of the log of oil, and pour it into the palm of his own left hand.
verses.explainer: The priest takes the log of oil and pours some into the palm of his own left hand.

verses.text: The priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand, and shall sprinkle some of the oil with his finger seven times before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: He dips his right forefinger into the oil in his left palm and sprinkles some of it seven times before the Lord.

verses.text: The priest shall put some of the rest of the oil that is in his hand on the tip of the right ear of him who is to be cleansed, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot, upon the blood of the trespass offering.
verses.explainer: The priest puts some of the remaining oil on the lobe of the right ear of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot, on top of the blood of the guilt offering. Oil over blood -- anointing over atonement. The blood purifies; the oil consecrates.

verses.text: The rest of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall put on the head of him who is to be cleansed, and the priest shall make atonement for him before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The rest of the oil the priest puts on the head of the one being cleansed, and the priest makes atonement for them before the Lord. Oil poured on the head -- the same anointing given to priests and kings. The healed person is restored to full dignity.

verses.text: “The priest shall offer the sin offering, and make atonement for him who is to be cleansed because of his uncleanness: and afterward he shall kill the burnt offering;
verses.explainer: The priest sacrifices the sin offering and makes atonement for the one being cleansed from their uncleanness. Afterward, he slaughters the burnt offering.

verses.text: and the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the meal offering on the altar. The priest shall make atonement for him, and he shall be clean.
verses.explainer: He offers the burnt offering and grain offering on the altar. The priest makes atonement for them, and they are clean. Three offerings (guilt, sin, burnt) plus oil anointing -- full restoration requires full atonement.

verses.text: “If he is poor, and can’t afford so much, then he shall take one male lamb for a trespass offering to be waved, to make atonement for him, and one tenth of an ephah of fine flour mingled with oil for a meal offering, and a log of oil;
verses.explainer: If the person is poor and cannot afford these, they take one male lamb as a guilt offering, together with a smaller grain offering and one log of oil. The economic sliding scale appears again for this elaborate ceremony.

verses.text: and two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, such as he is able to afford; and the one shall be a sin offering, and the other a burnt offering.
verses.explainer: And two doves or two young pigeons, which the person can afford, one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering. Birds replace lambs for the poor.

verses.text: “On the eighth day he shall bring them for his cleansing to the priest, to the door of the Tent of Meeting, before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: On the eighth day they bring them for their cleansing to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting before the Lord. Same timing, same location, same purpose -- only the cost differs.

verses.text: The priest shall take the lamb of the trespass offering, and the log of oil, and the priest shall wave them for a wave offering before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The priest takes the lamb for the guilt offering, together with the log of oil, and waves them before the Lord.

verses.text: He shall kill the lamb of the trespass offering. The priest shall take some of the blood of the trespass offering and put it on the tip of the right ear of him who is to be cleansed, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.
verses.explainer: He slaughters the lamb for the guilt offering and takes some of its blood and puts it on the lobe of the right ear of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot.

verses.text: The priest shall pour some of the oil into the palm of his own left hand;
verses.explainer: The priest pours some of the oil into the palm of his own left hand.

verses.text: and the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger some of the oil that is in his left hand seven times before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: With his right forefinger the priest sprinkles some of the oil from his left palm seven times before the Lord.

verses.text: Then the priest shall put some of the oil that is in his hand on the tip of the right ear of him who is to be cleansed, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot, on the place of the blood of the trespass offering.
verses.explainer: He puts some of the oil from his palm on the same three places: right ear, right thumb, right big toe, on top of the blood of the guilt offering.

verses.text: The rest of the oil that is in the priest’s hand he shall put on the head of him who is to be cleansed, to make atonement for him before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The rest of the oil the priest puts on the head of the one being cleansed, to make atonement for them before the Lord.

verses.text: He shall offer one of the turtledoves, or of the young pigeons, such as he is able to afford,
verses.explainer: He sacrifices the doves or young pigeons, one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, together with the grain offering.

verses.text: even such as he is able to afford, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering, with the meal offering. The priest shall make atonement for him who is to be cleansed before Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: The priest makes atonement before the Lord for the one being cleansed. These are the regulations for anyone who has a defiling skin disease and who cannot afford the regular offerings for their cleansing. The chapter ensures no healed person stays excluded because of poverty.

verses.text: This is the law for him in whom is the plague of leprosy, who is not able to afford the sacrifice for his cleansing.
verses.explainer: These are the regulations for anyone who has a defiling skin disease and who cannot afford the regular offerings for their cleansing.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses and Aaron about contamination in houses when they enter the land of Canaan. This regulation anticipates settled life -- the Israelites are still in the wilderness but are receiving laws for when they have permanent homes.

verses.text: “When you have come into the land of Canaan, which I give to you for a possession, and I put a spreading mildew in a house in the land of your possession,
verses.explainer: When I put a spreading mold in a house in the land you possess -- God says 'I put' the mold. Even contamination is under God's sovereign control. Some interpreters see this as a testing mechanism; others see it as natural processes attributed to divine providence.

verses.text: then he who owns the house shall come and tell the priest, saying, ‘There seems to me to be some sort of plague in the house.’
verses.explainer: The owner of the house must go and tell the priest about what seems to be a contamination in the house.

verses.text: The priest shall command that they empty the house, before the priest goes in to examine the plague, that all that is in the house not be made unclean. Afterward the priest shall go in to inspect the house.
verses.explainer: The priest orders the house to be emptied before he goes in to examine the contamination, so that nothing in the house will be pronounced unclean. Practical concern: if items are removed before the examination, they are not contaminated by the declaration.

verses.text: He shall examine the plague; and behold, if the plague is in the walls of the house with hollow streaks, greenish or reddish, and it appears to be deeper than the wall;
verses.explainer: He examines the contamination on the walls. If it has greenish or reddish depressions that appear deeper than the wall surface.

verses.text: then the priest shall go out of the house to the door of the house, and shut up the house seven days.
verses.explainer: The priest goes out to the doorway of the house and closes it up for seven days. Same quarantine period as for skin diseases.

verses.text: The priest shall come again on the seventh day, and look. If the plague has spread in the walls of the house,
verses.explainer: On the seventh day the priest returns to inspect. If the mold has spread on the walls.

verses.text: then the priest shall command that they take out the stones in which is the plague, and cast them into an unclean place outside of the city:
verses.explainer: He orders the contaminated stones to be torn out and thrown into an unclean place outside the town.

verses.text: and he shall cause the inside of the house to be scraped all over, and they shall pour out the mortar, that they scraped off, outside of the city into an unclean place.
verses.explainer: He has the inside walls of the house scraped and the plaster that is scraped off dumped in an unclean place outside the town.

verses.text: They shall take other stones, and put them in the place of those stones; and he shall take other mortar, and shall plaster the house.
verses.explainer: New stones replace the old ones, and new plaster is applied to the house.

verses.text: “If the plague comes again, and breaks out in the house, after he has taken out the stones, and after he has scraped the house, and after it was plastered;
verses.explainer: If the mold reappears in the house after the stones have been torn out and the house scraped and replastered.

verses.text: then the priest shall come in and look; and behold, if the plague has spread in the house, it is a destructive mildew in the house. It is unclean.
verses.explainer: The priest examines it. If the mold has spread, it is a persistent, defiling mold. The house is unclean.

verses.text: He shall break down the house, its stones, and its timber, and all the house’s mortar. He shall carry them out of the city into an unclean place.
verses.explainer: The house must be torn down -- its stones, timbers, and all the plaster -- and all of it taken to an unclean place outside the town. When contamination persists despite remediation, total destruction is the only option.

thenVsNow.then: Demolishing a contaminated house protected the community from the spread of mold and its health effects. The community's health took precedence over individual property rights.
thenVsNow.now: Modern building codes require similar measures for persistent mold contamination -- remediation first, then demolition if remediation fails. The ancient public health principle remains sound.

verses.text: “Moreover he who goes into the house while it is shut up shall be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone who goes into the house while it is closed up will be unclean until evening.

verses.text: He who lies down in the house shall wash his clothes; and he who eats in the house shall wash his clothes.
verses.explainer: Anyone who sleeps or eats in the house must wash their clothes.

verses.text: “If the priest shall come in, and examine it, and behold, the plague hasn’t spread in the house, after the house was plastered, then the priest shall pronounce the house clean, because the plague is healed.
verses.explainer: But if the priest comes and examines the house and the mold has not spread after the house was replastered, the priest pronounces the house clean because the mold is gone.

verses.text: To cleanse the house he shall take two birds, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop.
verses.explainer: To purify the house, he takes two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop.

verses.text: He shall kill one of the birds in an earthen vessel over running water.
verses.explainer: He kills one of the birds over fresh water in a clay pot.

verses.text: He shall take the cedar wood, and the hyssop, and the scarlet, and the living bird, and dip them in the blood of the slain bird, and in the running water, and sprinkle the house seven times.
verses.explainer: He takes the cedar wood, hyssop, scarlet yarn, and the live bird, dips them in the blood of the dead bird and the fresh water, and sprinkles the house seven times.

verses.text: He shall cleanse the house with the blood of the bird, and with the running water, with the living bird, with the cedar wood, with the hyssop, and with the scarlet;
verses.explainer: He purifies the house with the bird's blood, the fresh water, the live bird, the cedar wood, the hyssop, and the scarlet yarn.

verses.text: but he shall let the living bird go out of the city into the open field. So shall he make atonement for the house; and it shall be clean.”
verses.explainer: He releases the live bird in the open field outside the town. He makes atonement for the house, and it is clean. The same two-bird ceremony used for healed people is used for healed houses -- purification works the same way for structures as for bodies.

verses.text: This is the law for any plague of leprosy, and for an itch,
verses.explainer: These are the regulations for any defiling skin disease, for a sore.

verses.text: and for the destructive mildew of a garment, and for a house,
verses.explainer: For defiling molds in fabric or in a house.

verses.text: and for a rising, and for a scab, and for a bright spot;
verses.explainer: And for a swelling, rash, or shiny spot.

verses.text:
to teach when it is unclean, and when it is clean.
This is the law of leprosy.
verses.explainer: To determine when something is clean or unclean. These are the regulations for defiling skin diseases and defiling molds. The summary closes this long section with its governing principle: distinguishing clean from unclean.

explainer.summary: This chapter addresses bodily discharges that cause ritual uncleanness: abnormal male discharge, seminal emission, menstruation, and abnormal female bleeding. The contamination extends to anyone or anything the affected person touches. The laws are symmetrical -- men and women are treated with parallel structures. The chapter is not about shame or punishment but about maintaining appropriate boundaries around the mystery of life-generating substances.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 completes the purity laws that began in chapter 11. Together, these chapters cover food (11), childbirth (12), skin diseases (13-14), and bodily discharges (15). Every aspect of physical existence falls under the holiness framework.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses and Aaron about bodily discharges.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘When any man has a discharge from his body, because of his discharge he is unclean.
verses.explainer: When any man has a bodily discharge, the discharge is unclean. This likely refers to a pathological discharge, not normal bodily function.

verses.text: This shall be his uncleanness in his discharge: whether his body runs with his discharge, or his body has stopped from his discharge, it is his uncleanness.
verses.explainer: Whether his body releases the discharge or blocks it, it is his uncleanness. Either condition renders him unclean.

verses.text: “‘Every bed whereon he who has the discharge lies shall be unclean; and everything he sits on shall be unclean.
verses.explainer: Any bed the man with a discharge lies on will be unclean, and anything he sits on will be unclean. Contamination transfers to furniture through contact.

verses.text: Whoever touches his bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone who touches his bed must wash their clothes and bathe with water, and will be unclean till evening. Secondary contamination through contact with contaminated objects.

verses.text: He who sits on anything whereon the man who has the discharge sat shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Whoever sits on anything the man sat on must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘He who touches the body of him who has the discharge shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Whoever touches the man must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘If he who has the discharge spits on him who is clean, then he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: If the man with the discharge spits on someone who is clean, that person must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘Whatever saddle he who has the discharge rides on shall be unclean.
verses.explainer: Everything the man rides on will be unclean.

verses.text: Whoever touches anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening. He who carries those things shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Whoever touches anything that was under him will be unclean till evening; whoever picks these things up must wash their clothes and bathe.

verses.text: “‘Whoever he who has the discharge touches, without having rinsed his hands in water, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone the man touches without rinsing his hands must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘The earthen vessel, which he who has the discharge touches, shall be broken; and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water.
verses.explainer: A clay pot the man touches must be broken, and any wooden article must be rinsed. The same clay/wood distinction as in 6:28 and 11:33.

verses.text: “‘When he who has a discharge is cleansed of his discharge, then he shall count to himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes; and he shall bathe his flesh in running water, and shall be clean.
verses.explainer: When a man is cleansed from his discharge, he counts off seven days for his ceremonial cleansing, washes his clothes, and bathes in fresh water. Then he is clean.

verses.text: “‘On the eighth day he shall take two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, and come before Yahweh to the door of the Tent of Meeting, and give them to the priest:
verses.explainer: On the eighth day he takes two doves or two young pigeons and comes before the Lord at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting and gives them to the priest.

verses.text: and the priest shall offer them, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering. The priest shall make atonement for him before Yahweh for his discharge.
verses.explainer: The priest sacrifices them, one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. He makes atonement before the Lord for the man because of his discharge. The same sin-and-burnt-offering pair used for other purification rituals.

verses.text: “‘If any man has an emission of semen, then he shall bathe all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: When a man has an emission of semen, he must bathe his whole body with water and will be unclean till evening. Normal seminal emission causes only minor, temporary uncleanness.

verses.text: Every garment, and every skin, whereon the semen is, shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Any clothing or leather that has semen on it must be washed with water, unclean till evening.

verses.text: If a man lies with a woman and there is an emission of semen, they shall both bathe themselves in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: When a man has sexual relations with a woman and there is an emission of semen, both must bathe with water, unclean till evening. Both partners share the temporary uncleanness. Sexual intercourse within marriage is not sinful but produces a brief ritual status change.

thenVsNow.then: The temporary uncleanness from sexual intercourse meant that couples could not participate in worship immediately afterward. Sex was not wrong but belonged to a different sphere than worship -- the two activities required different states of being.
thenVsNow.now: The idea that certain activities are appropriate in their own context but require transition before entering sacred space persists in many traditions. It is not about shame but about recognizing different modes of human experience.

verses.text: “‘If a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her flesh is blood, she shall be in her impurity seven days: and whoever touches her shall be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: When a woman has her regular flow of blood, the impurity of her monthly period lasts seven days. Anyone who touches her will be unclean till evening. Menstruation causes a seven-day ritual status that parallels the male discharge rules.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Menstrual impurity', 'explanation': 'The seven-day period was about blood flow, not about women being inferior. Blood represented life, and its loss signified a brush with death in Israelite thought. The laws protected the boundary between life and death, not a boundary between genders. Men with comparable discharges received the same treatment.'}

verses.text: “‘Everything that she lies on in her impurity shall be unclean. Everything also that she sits on shall be unclean.
verses.explainer: Anything she lies on during her period will be unclean, and anything she sits on will be unclean.

verses.text: Whoever touches her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone who touches her bed must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: Whoever touches anything that she sits on shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone who touches anything she sits on must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: If it is on the bed, or on anything whereon she sits, when he touches it, he shall be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Whether it is the bed or anything she sits on, when anyone touches it, they are unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘If any man lies with her, and her monthly flow is on him, he shall be unclean seven days; and every bed whereon he lies shall be unclean.
verses.explainer: If a man lies with her and her monthly flow touches him, he will be unclean for seven days, and any bed he lies on will be unclean.

verses.text: “‘If a woman has a discharge of her blood many days not in the time of her period, or if she has a discharge beyond the time of her period; all the days of the discharge of her uncleanness shall be as in the days of her period: she is unclean.
verses.explainer: When a woman has a discharge of blood for many days at a time other than her monthly period, or when it lasts beyond her period, she is unclean as long as it continues. This is the condition of the woman who touched Jesus' cloak in Mark 5:25-34 -- twelve years of uncleanness.

verses.text: Every bed whereon she lies all the days of her discharge shall be to her as the bed of her period: and everything whereon she sits shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her period.
verses.explainer: Any bed she lies on while her discharge continues will be unclean, and anything she sits on will be unclean.

verses.text: Whoever touches these things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening.
verses.explainer: Anyone who touches these things will be unclean; they must wash their clothes and bathe, unclean till evening.

verses.text: “‘But if she is cleansed of her discharge, then she shall count to herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean.
verses.explainer: When she is cleansed from her discharge, she must count off seven days and after that she is ceremonially clean.

verses.text: On the eighth day she shall take two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, and bring them to the priest, to the door of the Tent of Meeting.
verses.explainer: On the eighth day she takes two doves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: The priest shall offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make atonement for her before Yahweh for the uncleanness of her discharge.
verses.explainer: The priest sacrifices one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. He makes atonement for her before the Lord for the uncleanness of her discharge. The same offering pair as the male discharge -- gender symmetry in the purification system.

verses.text: “‘Thus you shall separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness, so they will not die in their uncleanness, when they defile my tabernacle that is among them.’”
verses.explainer: You must keep the Israelites separate from things that make them unclean, so they will not die in their uncleanness for defiling my dwelling place, which is among them. The ultimate purpose of all purity laws: protecting God's dwelling from contamination so that his presence can remain among Israel.

thenVsNow.then: God's presence in the Tabernacle was both Israel's greatest blessing and greatest danger. An unclean person entering the sanctuary could provoke divine judgment. The purity laws kept the people safe.
thenVsNow.now: The New Testament reinterprets these laws through Christ. The 'temple' is now the believing community (1 Corinthians 3:16) and the individual body (1 Corinthians 6:19). Holiness shifts from ritual categories to moral and spiritual categories.

verses.text: This is the law of him who has a discharge, and of him who has an emission of semen, so that he is unclean thereby;
verses.explainer: These are the regulations for a man with a discharge, for anyone made unclean by an emission of semen.

verses.text: and of her who has her period, and of a man or woman who has a discharge, and of him who lies with her who is unclean.
verses.explainer: For a woman in her monthly period, for a man or woman with a discharge, and for a man who has sexual relations with a woman who is ceremonially unclean. The summary covers all the discharge categories symmetrically.

explainer.summary: This is the theological center of Leviticus and one of the most important chapters in the Old Testament. The Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) is the annual ceremony when the high priest enters the Holy of Holies to make atonement for all Israel's sins. It involves two goats: one is sacrificed as a sin offering, and the other -- the scapegoat -- is sent alive into the wilderness, symbolically carrying away the nation's sins. This is the only day of the year anyone may enter God's innermost presence, and the ritual is performed by the high priest alone.
themes: The high priest entering God's presence
Two goats -- one sacrificed, one sent away
Annual purification of the entire nation
The mercy seat as the place of atonement
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 16 stands at the heart of Leviticus, both structurally and theologically. Everything before it builds toward this moment (the sacrificial system, priestly consecration, purity laws), and everything after it flows from it (the Holiness Code in chapters 17-26). The Day of Atonement is the mechanism that makes continued relationship between holy God and sinful Israel possible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The gold lid of the Ark of the Covenant between th
wordStudies.meaning: The gold lid of the Ark of the Covenant between the two cherubim. From the same root as 'atonement' (kaphar). It is where God's presence dwells and where blood is sprinkled on the Day of Atonement. It is literally the place where atonement happens in God's presence.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One of the most debated words in the Hebrew Bible
wordStudies.meaning: One of the most debated words in the Hebrew Bible. Interpretations include: a place name (a cliff in the wilderness), a demon of the wasteland, or an abstract term meaning 'complete removal.' Whatever the exact meaning, the goat sent to 'azazel' carries Israel's sins away into uninhabited wilderness -- removal rather than just covering.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they came near before Yahweh, and died;
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses after the death of Aaron's two sons who died when they approached the Lord. The Day of Atonement regulations are directly connected to Nadab and Abihu's fatal unauthorized approach. Their death is the reason for strict protocols.

verses.text: and Yahweh said to Moses, “Tell Aaron your brother, not to come at all times into the Most Holy Place within the veil, before the mercy seat which is on the ark; lest he die: for I will appear in the cloud on the mercy seat.
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses to tell Aaron not to come whenever he chooses into the Most Holy Place behind the curtain, in front of the atonement cover on the ark, or he will die. For the Lord appears in the cloud over the atonement cover. Access to God's most intimate presence is restricted to one person, one day, one specific ritual. Anything else is fatal.

thenVsNow.then: The curtain separating the Holy of Holies from the rest of the Tabernacle was a physical barrier representing the spiritual barrier between sinful humanity and holy God. Only atoning blood could allow passage.
thenVsNow.now: Christians believe the curtain was torn from top to bottom when Jesus died (Mark 15:38), symbolizing that his death opened permanent access to God's presence. The book of Hebrews develops this extensively, presenting Jesus as the ultimate high priest who enters the true Holy of Holies once for all.

verses.text: “Aaron shall come into the sanctuary with a young bull for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering.
verses.explainer: This is how Aaron is to enter the Most Holy Place: with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering. He must first atone for himself before atoning for the people.

verses.text: He shall put on the holy linen coat. He shall have the linen breeches on his body, and shall put on the linen sash, and he shall be clothed with the linen turban. They are the holy garments. He shall bathe his body in water, and put them on.
verses.explainer: He is to put on a sacred linen tunic, with linen undergarments, a linen sash, and a linen turban. These are sacred garments. He must bathe and then put them on. On this day alone, the high priest sets aside his elaborate gold-and-blue garments and wears simple white linen. He enters God's presence not in his impressive regalia but in humble, plain clothes.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'White linen garments', 'explanation': "The high priest's regular garments included gold, precious stones, and colored fabric symbolizing glory and authority. On the Day of Atonement, he wore only plain white linen -- the simplest priestly garment. Before God in the Holy of Holies, even the high priest stands as a humble supplicant, not a magnificent leader."}

verses.text: He shall take from the congregation of the children of Israel two male goats for a sin offering, and one ram for a burnt offering.
verses.explainer: From the Israelite community he takes two male goats for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering. The community provides the goats -- this is a national, not just priestly, ceremony.

verses.text: “Aaron shall offer the bull of the sin offering, which is for himself, and make atonement for himself and for his house.
verses.explainer: Aaron is to offer the bull for his own sin offering to make atonement for himself and his household.

verses.text: He shall take the two goats, and set them before Yahweh at the door of the Tent of Meeting.
verses.explainer: Then he takes the two goats and presents them before the Lord at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: Aaron shall cast lots for the two goats; one lot for Yahweh, and the other lot for the scapegoat.
verses.explainer: He casts lots for the two goats -- one lot for the Lord and the other for the scapegoat (azazel). The lots remove human choice from the equation. God determines which goat lives and which dies.

verses.text: Aaron shall present the goat on which the lot fell for Yahweh, and offer him for a sin offering.
verses.explainer: Aaron brings the goat whose lot falls to the Lord and sacrifices it as a sin offering.

verses.text: But the goat on which the lot fell for the scapegoat shall be presented alive before Yahweh, to make atonement for him, to send him away as the scapegoat into the wilderness.
verses.explainer: But the goat chosen by lot as the scapegoat shall be presented alive before the Lord to be used for making atonement by sending it into the wilderness as a scapegoat. The live goat is not sacrificed but sent away -- a living visual of sin being carried off and removed.

verses.text: “Aaron shall present the bull of the sin offering, which is for himself, and shall make atonement for himself and for his house, and shall kill the bull of the sin offering which is for himself.
verses.explainer: Aaron offers the bull as a sin offering for himself and makes atonement for himself and his household.

verses.text: He shall take a censer full of coals of fire from off the altar before Yahweh, and two handfuls of sweet incense beaten small, and bring it within the veil:
verses.explainer: He takes a censer full of burning coals from the altar before the Lord and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense and brings them behind the curtain. This is the moment: the high priest passes through the curtain into the Holy of Holies.

verses.text: and he shall put the incense on the fire before Yahweh, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is on the testimony, so that he will not die.
verses.explainer: He puts the incense on the fire before the Lord, and the cloud of incense conceals the atonement cover above the ark so that he will not die. The incense cloud serves as a screen between the priest and God's direct presence -- even in the moment of closest approach, God's full glory is shielded.

verses.text: He shall take some of the blood of the bull, and sprinkle it with his finger on the mercy seat on the east; and before the mercy seat he shall sprinkle some of the blood with his finger seven times.
verses.explainer: He takes some of the bull's blood and with his finger sprinkles it on the front of the atonement cover. Then he sprinkles blood with his finger seven times before the atonement cover. Blood is applied directly to the mercy seat -- the very throne of God. This is the most sacred act of the most sacred day in Israel's calendar.

verses.text: “Then he shall kill the goat of the sin offering, that is for the people, and bring his blood within the veil, and do with his blood as he did with the blood of the bull, and sprinkle it on the mercy seat, and before the mercy seat.
verses.explainer: He slaughters the goat for the sin offering for the people and brings its blood behind the curtain. He does with it as he did with the bull's blood: sprinkles it on the atonement cover and in front of it. The people's sin offering blood follows the priest's into the Holy of Holies.

verses.text: He shall make atonement for the Holy Place, because of the uncleanness of the children of Israel, and because of their transgressions, even all their sins; and so he shall do for the Tent of Meeting, that dwells with them in the middle of their uncleanness.
verses.explainer: In this way he makes atonement for the Most Holy Place because of the uncleanness and rebellion of the Israelites, whatever their sins have been. He does the same for the Tent of Meeting, which is among them in the midst of their uncleanness. The blood purifies the sanctuary itself -- Israel's sins have contaminated God's dwelling, and the Day of Atonement cleanses it.

thenVsNow.then: The theology here is that sin pollutes not just the sinner but God's dwelling. Every sin committed throughout the year deposits contamination on the sanctuary. The Day of Atonement is the annual deep cleaning of God's house.
thenVsNow.now: This 'pollution' theology influences the Christian understanding of sin as something that damages the relationship between God and humanity, not just a legal infraction. Sin has real consequences that require real remedy.

verses.text: No one shall be in the Tent of Meeting when he enters to make atonement in the Holy Place, until he comes out, and has made atonement for himself and for his household, and for all the assembly of Israel.
verses.explainer: No one is to be in the Tent of Meeting from the time Aaron goes in to make atonement in the Most Holy Place until he comes out, having made atonement for himself, his household, and the whole community of Israel. The high priest is utterly alone with God. No witness, no assistance. The fate of the nation rests on one man performing one ritual correctly.

verses.text: “He shall go out to the altar that is before Yahweh and make atonement for it, and shall take some of the bull’s blood, and some of the goat’s blood, and put it around on the horns of the altar.
verses.explainer: He comes out to the altar that is before the Lord and makes atonement for it. He takes some of the bull's blood and some of the goat's blood and puts it on all the horns of the altar.

verses.text: He shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it, and make it holy from the uncleanness of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: He sprinkles blood on it with his finger seven times to cleanse it and to consecrate it from the uncleanness of the Israelites. The altar, like the Holy of Holies, accumulates contamination from Israel's sins throughout the year and must be purified.

verses.text: “When he has finished atoning for the Holy Place, the Tent of Meeting, and the altar, he shall present the live goat.
verses.explainer: When Aaron has finished making atonement for the Most Holy Place, the Tent of Meeting, and the altar, he brings forward the live goat. Now the second phase begins -- the scapegoat ritual.

verses.text: Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat, and confess over him all the iniquities of the children of Israel, and all their transgressions, even all their sins; and he shall put them on the head of the goat, and shall send him away into the wilderness by the hand of a man who is in readiness.
verses.explainer: He lays both hands on the head of the live goat and confesses over it all the wickedness and rebellion of the Israelites -- all their sins -- and puts them on the goat's head. He sends the goat away into the wilderness in the care of someone appointed for the task. This is the most vivid picture of sin removal in the Old Testament. The high priest verbally transfers the nation's sins to an innocent animal, which then carries them away into the wasteland.

verses.text: The goat shall carry all their iniquities on himself to a solitary land, and he shall release the goat in the wilderness.
verses.explainer: The goat will carry on itself all their sins to a remote place, and the man shall release it in the wilderness. The sins go away -- physically, visibly, permanently removed from the community. The goat disappears into the desert, and the sins go with it.

thenVsNow.then: The people watched the scapegoat disappear into the wilderness, knowing it carried their sins with it. The visual was psychologically powerful -- guilt being physically removed from sight.
thenVsNow.now: The English word 'scapegoat' entered the language from this passage (via William Tyndale's translation). It now means anyone who unfairly bears blame for others. The original scapegoat, however, was not unfairly treated -- it was divinely appointed to represent the removal of real guilt.

verses.text: “Aaron shall come into the Tent of Meeting, and shall take off the linen garments, which he put on when he went into the Holy Place, and shall leave them there.
verses.explainer: Aaron enters the Tent of Meeting, takes off the linen garments he put on before entering the Most Holy Place, and leaves them there. The white linen garments are not reused. They were for this one sacred encounter.

verses.text: Then he shall bathe himself in water in a holy place, and put on his garments, and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people, and make atonement for himself and for the people.
verses.explainer: He bathes in water in the holy place and puts on his regular garments. Then he comes out and sacrifices the burnt offering for himself and the burnt offering for the people, making atonement for himself and for the people. The high priest returns to his regular vestments and completes the ceremony with burnt offerings.

verses.text: The fat of the sin offering he shall burn on the altar.
verses.explainer: He also burns the fat of the sin offering on the altar.

verses.text: “He who lets the goat go as the scapegoat shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp.
verses.explainer: The man who releases the goat as the scapegoat must wash his clothes and bathe before he comes back into the camp. Contact with the sin-bearing goat causes temporary uncleanness.

verses.text: The bull for the sin offering, and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the Holy Place, shall be carried outside the camp; and they shall burn their skins, their flesh, and their dung with fire.
verses.explainer: The bull and the goat for the sin offerings, whose blood was brought into the Most Holy Place to make atonement, must be taken outside the camp. Their hides, flesh, and intestines are to be burned up.

verses.text: He who burns them shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp.
verses.explainer: The man who burns them must wash his clothes and bathe before he comes back into the camp.

verses.text: “It shall be a statute to you forever: in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you shall afflict your souls, and shall do no kind of work, the native-born, or the stranger who lives as a foreigner among you:
verses.explainer: This is to be a lasting ordinance for you: On the tenth day of the seventh month you must deny yourselves and not do any work -- whether native-born or a foreigner residing among you. 'Deny yourselves' means fasting. This is the only mandated fast day in the Torah.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Deny yourselves', 'explanation': "Hebrew 'innah nephesh' -- to afflict or humble your soul. This came to mean fasting from food and drink, abstaining from washing, wearing leather shoes, using oils, and sexual relations. It was a day of total self-denial, reflecting the gravity of national sin."}

verses.text: for on this day shall atonement be made for you, to cleanse you; from all your sins you shall be clean before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Because on this day atonement will be made for you, to cleanse you. Then, before the Lord, you will be clean from all your sins. This verse states the purpose in its most concentrated form: complete cleansing from all sins, in God's presence.

verses.text: It is a Sabbath of solemn rest to you, and you shall afflict your souls. It is a statute forever.
verses.explainer: It is a day of sabbath rest, and you must deny yourselves. It is a lasting ordinance. Yom Kippur is called a 'sabbath of sabbaths' -- the most restful rest, the most solemn cessation of activity.

verses.text: The priest, who is anointed and who is consecrated to be priest in his father’s place, shall make the atonement, and shall put on the linen garments, even the holy garments.
verses.explainer: The priest who is anointed and ordained to succeed his father as high priest is to make atonement. He puts on the sacred linen garments. The regulation extends beyond Aaron to all future high priests.

verses.text: Then he shall make atonement for the Holy Sanctuary; and he shall make atonement for the Tent of Meeting and for the altar; and he shall make atonement for the priests and for all the people of the assembly.
verses.explainer: He makes atonement for the Most Holy Place, for the Tent of Meeting and the altar, and for the priests and all the members of the community. The scope is total: the sanctuary, the priests, and the people. Every dimension of Israel's relationship with God is renewed.

verses.text:
“This shall be an everlasting statute for you, to make atonement for the children of Israel once in the year because of all their sins.”
It was done as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: This is to be a lasting ordinance for you: Atonement is to be made once a year for all the sins of the Israelites. And it was done, as the Lord commanded Moses. The chapter closes with obedience. The Day of Atonement was instituted and carried out exactly as God prescribed.

explainer.summary: Chapter 17 opens the 'Holiness Code' (chapters 17-26) with regulations about blood. All slaughter must happen at the Tabernacle -- no more freelance sacrifices in the open fields. The reason is profound: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives.' Blood is sacred because it represents life, and God has designated it specifically for atonement. Eating blood is absolutely forbidden because consuming life-blood would violate the sanctity of the atonement system.
themes: All sacrifice must be centralized at the Tabernacle
The life is in the blood
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 17 is the hinge between the first half of Leviticus (ritual laws, chapters 1-16) and the second half (the Holiness Code, chapters 17-26). It transitions from sanctuary regulations to ethical and social laws, using blood theology as the bridge.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Blood is the central symbol of Leviticus
wordStudies.meaning: Blood is the central symbol of Leviticus. It represents life itself (17:11). In the sacrificial system, blood is the substance that accomplishes atonement -- the bridge between human sin and divine forgiveness. Its sanctity explains why eating it is absolutely forbidden.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses with instructions about blood and sacrifice.

verses.text: “Speak to Aaron, and to his sons, and to all the children of Israel, and say to them: ‘This is the thing which Yahweh has commanded,
verses.explainer: These instructions are for Aaron, his sons, and all the Israelites -- the entire community, not just the priests.

verses.text: Whatever man there is of the house of Israel, who kills a bull, or lamb, or goat, in the camp, or who kills it outside the camp,
verses.explainer: Any Israelite who slaughters an ox, lamb, or goat in the camp or outside the camp -- all sacrificial animals must be brought to the central sanctuary.

verses.text: and hasn’t brought it to the door of the Tent of Meeting, to offer it as an offering to Yahweh before Yahweh’s tabernacle: blood shall be imputed to that man. He has shed blood; and that man shall be cut off from among his people.
verses.explainer: Instead of bringing it to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting to present it as an offering to the Lord before the Tabernacle, that person shall be considered guilty of bloodshed -- they have shed blood and must be cut off. Unauthorized slaughter is treated as murder. This sounds extreme, but the logic is that all animal life is sacred, and taking it requires God's authorization.

thenVsNow.then: Before this regulation, Israelites could slaughter animals anywhere. Now all slaughter is centralized at the Tabernacle, ensuring priestly oversight and preventing syncretistic practices (sacrificing to other gods in the open fields).
thenVsNow.now: After the Temple was destroyed in 70 AD, Judaism could no longer practice animal sacrifice. This passage helped transition Jewish practice toward prayer and Torah study as substitutes for sacrifice -- a transformation that continues today.

verses.text: This is to the end that the children of Israel may bring their sacrifices, which they sacrifice in the open field, that they may bring them to Yahweh, to the door of the Tent of Meeting, to the priest, and sacrifice them for sacrifices of peace offerings to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: This is so the Israelites will bring to the Lord the sacrifices they are now making in the open fields. They bring them to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and sacrifice them as peace offerings. The 'open field' sacrifices were unregulated and potentially directed toward other deities.

verses.text: The priest shall sprinkle the blood on Yahweh’s altar at the door of the Tent of Meeting, and burn the fat for a pleasant aroma to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The priest splashes the blood against the altar of the Lord at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and burns the fat as a pleasing aroma to the Lord.

verses.text: They shall no more sacrifice their sacrifices to the goat idols, after which they play the prostitute. This shall be a statute forever to them throughout their generations.’
verses.explainer: They must no longer offer any of their sacrifices to the goat idols to whom they prostitute themselves. This is a lasting ordinance for them and for the generations to come. The 'goat idols' (Hebrew se'irim, literally 'hairy ones') reveals what was actually happening: some Israelites were offering sacrifices to field demons or goat-spirits in the wilderness.

verses.text: “You shall say to them, ‘Any man there is of the house of Israel, or of the strangers who live as foreigners among them, who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice,
verses.explainer: Any Israelite or foreigner residing among them who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice without bringing it to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting to sacrifice it to the Lord must be cut off. Foreigners living in Israel are held to the same standard.

verses.text: and doesn’t bring it to the door of the Tent of Meeting, to sacrifice it to Yahweh; that man shall be cut off from his people.
verses.explainer: This applies to any Israelite or foreigner who offers a sacrifice -- it must come to the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: “‘Any man of the house of Israel, or of the strangers who live as foreigners among them, who eats any kind of blood, I will set my face against that soul who eats blood, and will cut him off from among his people.
verses.explainer: I will set my face against any Israelite or foreigner who eats blood, and I will cut them off from the people. God himself will enforce the blood prohibition -- he 'sets his face against' the violator. This is personal divine opposition.

verses.text: For the life of the flesh is in the blood; and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that makes atonement by reason of the life.
verses.explainer: For the life of a creature is in the blood, and I have given it to you to make atonement for yourselves on the altar; it is the blood that makes atonement for one's life. This is the most important theological statement in Leviticus. Three claims: (1) blood equals life, (2) God gave blood for atonement, and (3) blood atones because it represents life given in place of life.

thenVsNow.then: This verse is the theological foundation of the entire sacrificial system. Without it, animal sacrifice is just butchery. With it, sacrifice becomes the God-ordained mechanism for restoring the relationship between sinful humans and holy God.
thenVsNow.now: Christians read this verse as the foundation for understanding Jesus' death. When the New Testament says Christ's blood 'cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7) or that 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness' (Hebrews 9:22), it is building directly on Leviticus 17:11.

verses.text: Therefore I have said to the children of Israel, “No person among you may eat blood, nor may any stranger who lives as a foreigner among you eat blood.”
verses.explainer: Therefore I say to the Israelites: None of you may eat blood, nor may any foreigner residing among you eat blood.

verses.text: “‘Whatever man there is of the children of Israel, or of the strangers who live as foreigners among them, who takes in hunting any animal or bird that may be eaten; he shall pour out its blood, and cover it with dust.
verses.explainer: Any Israelite or foreigner who hunts any animal or bird that may be eaten must drain out the blood and cover it with earth. Even in hunting (outside the sacrificial context), blood must be treated with respect -- drained and buried.

verses.text: For as to the life of all flesh, its blood is with its life: therefore I said to the children of Israel, “You shall not eat the blood of any kind of flesh; for the life of all flesh is its blood. Whoever eats it shall be cut off.”
verses.explainer: Because the life of every creature is its blood. That is why I have said to the Israelites: You must not eat the blood of any creature, because the life of every creature is its blood; anyone who eats it must be cut off. The repetition drives the point home: blood is life, and life belongs to God.

verses.text: “‘Every person that eats what dies of itself, or that which is torn by animals, whether he is native-born or a foreigner, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the evening: then he shall be clean.
verses.explainer: Anyone, whether native-born or foreigner, who eats anything found dead or torn by wild animals must wash their clothes and bathe, and will be ceremonially unclean till evening. Then they will be clean.

verses.text: But if he doesn’t wash them, or bathe his flesh, then he shall bear his iniquity.’”
verses.explainer: But if they do not wash their clothes and bathe, they will be held responsible. The chapter ends with personal responsibility -- you are accountable for maintaining your own purity.

explainer.summary: Chapter 18 prohibits a comprehensive list of sexual practices: incest with various family members, sexual relations during menstruation, adultery, child sacrifice to Molech, homosexual acts, and bestiality. The chapter is framed by a warning not to follow the practices of Egypt (where they had lived) or Canaan (where they were going). The land itself is described as being defiled by these practices, and the Canaanites are 'vomited out' because of them. Israel must live differently to remain in the Promised Land.
themes: Rejection of Egyptian and Canaanite practices
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 18 begins the ethical section of the Holiness Code. After establishing blood theology (chapter 17), Leviticus turns to sexual ethics. Both blood and sex involve life at its most fundamental -- both require boundaries to preserve holiness.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A euphemism for sexual intercourse
wordStudies.meaning: A euphemism for sexual intercourse. 'Uncovering nakedness' in Leviticus always means sexual relations, not merely seeing someone undressed. The phrase carries connotations of violating boundaries and exposing what should be protected.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses with instructions about sexual conduct.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and say to them, ‘I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Speak to the Israelites and tell them: I am the Lord your God. The divine self-identification grounds the commands in relationship -- these are not arbitrary rules but the expectations of Israel's covenant God.

verses.text: You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived: and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, where I am bringing you. You shall not follow their statutes.
verses.explainer: You must not do as they do in Egypt, where you used to live, and you must not do as they do in the land of Canaan, where I am bringing you. Do not follow their practices. Israel is defined by what it does not do -- negatively distinguished from both its past (Egypt) and its future neighbors (Canaan).

thenVsNow.then: Egypt and Canaan both practiced forms of sexual conduct that Israel was to reject. Egyptian royal marriages often involved siblings, and Canaanite religion included ritual prostitution as a form of fertility worship.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that a faith community's sexual ethics should be distinctive from the surrounding culture remains one of the most debated aspects of religious life in the modern world. The tension between cultural norms and religious standards is ancient.

verses.text: You shall do my ordinances, and you shall keep my statutes, and walk in them: I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: You must obey my laws and be careful to follow my decrees. I am the Lord your God.

verses.text: You shall therefore keep my statutes and my ordinances; which if a man does, he shall live in them. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Keep my decrees and laws, for the person who obeys them will live by them. I am the Lord. The promise is life -- obedience leads to flourishing. These are not restrictions for restriction's sake but boundaries that create space for abundant life.

verses.text: “‘None of you shall approach any close relatives, to uncover their nakedness: I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: No one is to approach any close relative to have sexual relations. I am the Lord. The general principle: close family members are off limits. The 'I am the Lord' refrain appears throughout as the ultimate authority behind each command.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father, nor the nakedness of your mother: she is your mother. You shall not uncover her nakedness.
verses.explainer: Do not dishonor your father by having sexual relations with your mother. She is your mother; do not have relations with her.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father’s wife. It is your father’s nakedness.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your father's wife; that would dishonor your father. A father's wife (stepmother) is included in the prohibition.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your sister, the daughter of your father, or the daughter of your mother, whether born at home, or born abroad.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your sister, either your father's daughter or your mother's daughter, whether born in the same home or elsewhere. Full sisters and half-sisters are both prohibited.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your son’s daughter, or of your daughter’s daughter, even their nakedness: for theirs is your own nakedness.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your son's daughter or your daughter's daughter; that would dishonor you. Granddaughters are prohibited.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father’s wife’s daughter, conceived by your father, since she is your sister.
