verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with the daughter of your father's wife, born to your father; she is your sister. Step-sisters are included.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father’s sister: she is your father’s near kinswoman.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your father's sister; she is your father's close relative.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother’s sister: for she is your mother’s near kinswoman.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your mother's sister, because she is your mother's close relative.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father’s brother, you shall not approach his wife. She is your aunt.
verses.explainer: Do not dishonor your father's brother by approaching his wife to have sexual relations; she is your aunt.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your daughter-in-law: she is your son’s wife. You shall not uncover her nakedness.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your daughter-in-law. She is your son's wife; do not have relations with her.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your brother’s wife. It is your brother’s nakedness.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your brother's wife; that would dishonor your brother. An exception existed for levirate marriage (Deuteronomy 25:5-10) when a brother died without children.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter. You shall not take her son’s daughter, or her daughter’s daughter, to uncover her nakedness; they are near kinswomen: it is wickedness.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with both a woman and her daughter. Do not have sexual relations with either her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter; they are her close relatives. That is wickedness.

verses.text: “‘You shall not take a wife in addition to her sister, to be a rival, to uncover her nakedness, while her sister is still alive.
verses.explainer: Do not take your wife's sister as a rival wife and have sexual relations with her while your wife is living. This prohibits the specific practice of marrying two sisters simultaneously -- the situation that caused such pain in Jacob's household with Rachel and Leah.

verses.text: “‘You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness, as long as she is impure by her uncleanness.
verses.explainer: Do not approach a woman to have sexual relations during the uncleanness of her monthly period. This connects the sexual laws to the purity laws of chapter 15.

verses.text: “‘You shall not lie carnally with your neighbor’s wife, and defile yourself with her.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with your neighbor's wife and defile yourself with her. Adultery is prohibited -- the violation of another family's covenantal bond.

verses.text: “‘You shall not give any of your children as a sacrifice to Molech. You shall not profane the name of your God. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not give any of your children to be sacrificed to Molek, for you must not profane the name of your God. I am the Lord. Child sacrifice appears in a list of sexual sins because Molek worship involved both sexual rites and the burning of children. It represents the ultimate perversion of the life-giving purpose of sexuality.

verses.text: “‘You shall not lie with a man, as with a woman. That is detestable.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with a man as one does with a woman; that is detestable. This is one of the most debated verses in the Bible. In its original context, it is part of a list of sexual prohibitions that define Israel's distinctiveness from Canaanite practice.

verses.text: “‘You shall not lie with any animal to defile yourself with it. No woman may give herself to an animal, to lie down with it: it is a perversion.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with an animal and defile yourself with it. A woman must not present herself to an animal to have sexual relations with it; that is a perversion. Bestiality is prohibited for both men and women.

verses.text: “‘Don’t defile yourselves in any of these things: for in all these the nations which I am casting out before you were defiled.
verses.explainer: Do not defile yourselves in any of these ways, because this is how the nations that I am going to drive out before you became defiled. The nations already in Canaan practiced these things and lost their land as a result.

verses.text: The land was defiled: therefore I punished its iniquity, and the land vomited out her inhabitants.
verses.explainer: Even the land was defiled; so I punished it for its sin, and the land vomited out its inhabitants. The land itself is personified as rejecting its inhabitants' behavior. Sin has environmental and social consequences that go beyond the individual.

verses.text: You therefore shall keep my statutes and my ordinances, and shall not do any of these abominations; neither the native-born, nor the stranger who lives as a foreigner among you;
verses.explainer: But you must keep my decrees and my laws. The native-born and the foreigners residing among you must not do any of these detestable things.

verses.text: (for the men of the land that were before you had done all these abominations, and the land became defiled);
verses.explainer: For all these things were done by the people who lived in the land before you, and the land became defiled.

verses.text: that the land not vomit you out also, when you defile it, as it vomited out the nation that was before you.
verses.explainer: And if you defile the land, it will vomit you out as it vomited out the nations that were before you. Israel is not exempt from the same consequences. If they behave like the Canaanites, they will share the Canaanites' fate.

thenVsNow.then: This warning proved prophetic. The prophets later cited sexual immorality and child sacrifice among the reasons for Israel's exile from the land (Ezekiel 16, 23).
thenVsNow.now: The concept that land or society can be 'defiled' by its inhabitants' behavior resonates with modern concerns about the social fabric being damaged by systemic injustice and moral corruption.

verses.text: “‘For whoever shall do any of these abominations, even the souls that do them shall be cut off from among their people.
verses.explainer: Everyone who does any of these detestable things must be cut off from their people.

verses.text: Therefore you shall keep my requirements, that you do not practice any of these abominable customs, which were practiced before you, and that you do not defile yourselves with them. I am Yahweh your God.’”
verses.explainer: Keep my requirements and do not follow any of the detestable customs that were practiced before you came and do not defile yourselves with them. I am the Lord your God. The chapter ends as it began: with the Lord's self-identification as the ground of all ethical obligation.

explainer.summary: This is one of the most remarkable chapters in the entire Bible -- a sweeping vision of holiness that touches every area of daily life. It includes the command to 'love your neighbor as yourself,' prohibitions against stealing, lying, and oppression, instructions about wages, treatment of the disabled, justice in courts, and care for foreigners. The chapter demolishes any idea that holiness is only about rituals and sacrifices. To be holy as God is holy means treating other people with justice, compassion, and respect.
themes: Be holy as God is holy -- applied to daily life
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 19 is the ethical heart of the Holiness Code and arguably of the entire book of Leviticus. It shows that the same God who cares about sacrifice and purity cares equally about wages, honesty, and how you treat strangers.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Set apart, distinct, other
wordStudies.meaning: Set apart, distinct, other. Applied to God, it means he is utterly different from creation. Applied to people, it means living in a way that reflects God's character -- distinct from the surrounding culture in ethics, worship, and relationships.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Fellow human being, companion, associate
wordStudies.meaning: Fellow human being, companion, associate. In its original context, 'neighbor' primarily meant a fellow Israelite, but the chapter expands the concept to include foreigners (verse 34). Jesus would later expand it even further in the Parable of the Good Samaritan.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses with instructions for the entire assembly of Israel. This is addressed to everyone, not just priests.

verses.text: “Speak to all the congregation of the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘You shall be holy; for I, Yahweh your God, am holy.
verses.explainer: Be holy because I, the Lord your God, am holy. This is the theme statement for the entire Holiness Code. The command is grounded in God's character -- holiness is not arbitrary rule-keeping but reflecting who God is.

verses.text: “‘Each one of you shall respect his mother and his father. You shall keep my Sabbaths. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Each of you must respect your mother and father, and you must observe my Sabbaths. I am the Lord your God. Family honor and Sabbath observance -- two of the Ten Commandments -- open the practical holiness section.

thenVsNow.then: Respect for parents ensured social stability and the transmission of faith in a culture without written contracts, insurance, or retirement plans. Sabbath observance demonstrated trust in God's provision.
thenVsNow.now: The pairing of family respect and rest from work suggests that both relationships and rhythms of rest are foundational to a holy life. Neither workaholism nor family neglect is consistent with holiness.

verses.text: “‘Don’t turn to idols, nor make molten gods for yourselves. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not turn to idols or make metal gods for yourselves. I am the Lord your God. Idol worship is incompatible with holiness.

verses.text: “‘When you offer a sacrifice of peace offerings to Yahweh, you shall offer it so that you may be accepted.
verses.explainer: When you sacrifice a peace offering to the Lord, sacrifice it so it will be accepted on your behalf.

verses.text: It shall be eaten the same day you offer it, and on the next day: and if anything remains until the third day, it shall be burned with fire.
verses.explainer: It shall be eaten on the day you sacrifice it or on the next day. Anything left over until the third day must be burned.

verses.text: If it is eaten at all on the third day, it is an abomination. It will not be accepted;
verses.explainer: If any of it is eaten on the third day, it is impure and will not be accepted.

verses.text: but everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity, because he has profaned the holy thing of Yahweh, and that soul shall be cut off from his people.
verses.explainer: Whoever eats it will bear their iniquity because they have desecrated what is holy to the Lord. That person must be cut off.

verses.text: “‘When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not wholly reap the corners of your field, neither shall you gather the gleanings of your harvest.
verses.explainer: When you reap the harvest of your land, do not reap to the very edges of your field or gather the gleanings of your harvest. Leave them for the poor and the foreigner. I am the Lord your God. Holiness has an economic dimension: it means deliberately leaving something for those who have nothing. This is not charity; it is built into the economic system.

thenVsNow.then: In an agricultural economy, gleaning laws created a social safety net. The poor had the right to harvest what landowners left behind. It preserved dignity because the poor worked for their food rather than begging.
thenVsNow.now: Modern social safety nets -- food banks, unemployment insurance, welfare programs -- serve a similar function. The Levitical principle is that a just society does not allow its most vulnerable members to starve.

verses.text: You shall not glean your vineyard, neither shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the foreigner. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not go over your vineyard a second time or pick up the grapes that have fallen. Leave them for the poor and the foreigner. I am the Lord your God. Vineyards as well as grain fields must leave portions for the poor.

verses.text:
“‘You shall not steal.
“‘You shall not lie.
“‘You shall not deceive one another.
verses.explainer: Do not steal. Do not lie. Do not deceive one another. Three prohibitions in one verse: theft, dishonesty, and deception. Holiness requires integrity in all dealings.

verses.text: “‘You shall not swear by my name falsely, and profane the name of your God. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not swear falsely by my name and so profane the name of your God. I am the Lord. Using God's name in a false oath damages God's reputation and undermines the entire oath system that undergirds society.

verses.text:
“‘You shall not oppress your neighbor, nor rob him.
“‘The wages of a hired servant shall not remain with you all night until the morning.
verses.explainer: Do not defraud or rob your neighbor. Do not hold back the wages of a hired worker overnight. Wage theft is treated as seriously as robbery. A day laborer depended on daily wages to feed his family that night.

thenVsNow.then: Day laborers were among the most vulnerable workers in the ancient economy. Without wages, they and their families would go hungry that night. Delaying payment was not a financial inconvenience but a survival crisis.
thenVsNow.now: Wage theft remains one of the most common forms of labor exploitation worldwide. The principle that workers deserve prompt, fair payment is a core biblical ethical standard.

verses.text: “‘You shall not curse the deaf, nor put a stumbling block before the blind; but you shall fear your God. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block in front of the blind, but fear your God. I am the Lord. Exploiting someone's disability -- even when they cannot detect it -- is forbidden. God sees what the deaf cannot hear and what the blind cannot see.

thenVsNow.then: Cursing the deaf and tripping the blind are examples of exploiting vulnerability. Since the victim cannot detect the offense, only the fear of God prevents it.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is the foundation of disability rights in the biblical tradition. It establishes the principle that the vulnerable deserve protection, not exploitation, and that morality is measured by how you treat those who cannot fight back.

verses.text: “‘You shall do no injustice in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor, nor show favoritism to the great; but you shall judge your neighbor in righteousness.
verses.explainer: Do not pervert justice; do not show partiality to the poor or favoritism to the great, but judge your neighbor fairly. Justice must be impartial -- neither biased toward the rich (corruption) nor toward the poor (populism). Both are distortions.

verses.text:
“‘You shall not go up and down as a slanderer among your people.
“‘You shall not endanger the life of your neighbor. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not go about spreading slander among your people. Do not do anything that endangers your neighbor's life. I am the Lord. Gossip and endangering life are placed side by side, suggesting that slander can be as destructive as physical violence.

verses.text: “‘You shall not hate your brother in your heart. You shall surely rebuke your neighbor, and not bear sin because of him.
verses.explainer: Do not hate a fellow Israelite in your heart. Rebuke your neighbor frankly so you will not share in their guilt. The prohibition is against internal hatred -- you can be angry but you must address it directly rather than let it fester. Honest confrontation is preferable to silent resentment.

verses.text: “‘You shall not take vengeance, nor bear any grudge against the children of your people; but you shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not seek revenge or bear a grudge against anyone among your people, but love your neighbor as yourself. I am the Lord. This is the verse Jesus called the second greatest commandment. It is remarkable that it appears in Leviticus -- the book most people consider irrelevant to modern life contains the most quoted ethical command in the Bible.

thenVsNow.then: In its original context, 'neighbor' primarily meant a fellow Israelite, though verse 34 extends love to the foreigner as well. The command was already radical in requiring positive love, not just the absence of harm.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus expanded 'neighbor' to include enemies and strangers (Luke 10:25-37). The command has become the foundation of Western ethics and human rights discourse, though its radical implications continue to challenge every culture.

verses.text:
“‘You shall keep my statutes.
“‘You shall not cross-breed different kinds of animals.
“‘You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed;
“‘Don’t wear a garment made of two kinds of material.
verses.explainer: Keep my decrees. Do not mate different kinds of animals. Do not plant your field with two kinds of seed. Do not wear clothing woven of two kinds of material. These 'mixture' laws symbolize the broader principle of maintaining distinctions. Israel must not blur the boundaries God has established -- between species, between kinds of seed, between types of fabric. The practical effect was to constantly remind Israel of their calling to be separate.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Prohibition of mixtures', 'explanation': "These laws are often puzzling to modern readers. They likely symbolized Israel's calling to remain distinct and unmixed with surrounding nations. Just as fabrics, seeds, and animals were to be kept distinct, Israel was to maintain its identity. The blending of categories represented the corruption of God-ordained order."}

verses.text: “‘If a man lies carnally with a woman who is a slave girl, pledged to be married to another man, and not ransomed, or given her freedom; they shall be punished. They shall not be put to death, because she was not free.
verses.explainer: If a man sleeps with a female slave who is promised to another man but has not been redeemed or given her freedom, there must be punishment. But since she has not been freed, they are not to be put to death.

verses.text: He shall bring his trespass offering to Yahweh, to the door of the Tent of Meeting, even a ram for a trespass offering.
verses.explainer: The man must bring a ram as a guilt offering to the Lord, to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: The priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering before Yahweh for his sin which he has committed: and the sin which he has committed shall be forgiven him.
verses.explainer: With the ram the priest makes atonement for him before the Lord for the sin he committed, and his sin will be forgiven.

verses.text: “‘When you come into the land, and have planted all kinds of trees for food, then you shall count their fruit as forbidden. Three years they shall be forbidden to you. It shall not be eaten.
verses.explainer: When you enter the land and plant any kind of fruit tree, regard its fruit as forbidden. For three years you are to consider it forbidden; it must not be eaten.

verses.text: But in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy, for giving praise to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: In the fourth year all its fruit will be holy, an offering of praise to the Lord. The first productive harvest belongs to God.

verses.text: In the fifth year you shall eat its fruit, that it may yield its increase to you. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: In the fifth year you may eat its fruit. In this way your harvest will be increased. I am the Lord your God. The five-year cycle teaches patience and dedication of firstfruits.

verses.text: “‘You shall not eat any meat with the blood still in it. You shall not use enchantments, nor practice sorcery.
verses.explainer: Do not eat meat with the blood still in it. Do not practice divination or seek omens. Blood prohibition paired with divination prohibition -- both attempt to manipulate forces that belong to God alone.

verses.text: “‘You shall not cut the hair on the sides of your head or clip off the edge of your beard.
verses.explainer: Do not cut the hair at the sides of your head or clip off the edges of your beard. These grooming prohibitions may relate to mourning practices associated with pagan worship.

verses.text: “‘You shall not make any cuttings in your flesh for the dead, nor tattoo any marks on you. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not cut your bodies for the dead or put tattoo marks on yourselves. I am the Lord. Self-mutilation for the dead and tattooing were associated with pagan mourning rites and identification with other deities.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Tattoo marks', 'explanation': "The Hebrew word qa'aqa likely refers to incisions filled with pigment as part of mourning or pagan religious practices. The prohibition is specifically against marks made 'for the dead' -- connected to ancestor worship and pagan mourning rituals rather than decorative body art in general."}

verses.text: “‘Don’t profane your daughter, to make her a prostitute; lest the land fall to prostitution, and the land become full of wickedness.
verses.explainer: Do not degrade your daughter by making her a prostitute, or the land will turn to prostitution and be filled with wickedness. Fathers are forbidden from exploiting their daughters sexually for economic gain.

verses.text: “‘You shall keep my Sabbaths, and reverence my sanctuary; I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Observe my Sabbaths and have reverence for my sanctuary. I am the Lord. Sabbath and sanctuary -- time and space set apart for God.

verses.text: “‘Don’t turn to those who are mediums, nor to the wizards. Don’t seek them out, to be defiled by them. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not turn to mediums or seek out spiritists, for you will be defiled by them. I am the Lord your God. Consulting the dead or spirits is forbidden because it seeks guidance from sources other than God.

verses.text: “‘You shall rise up before the gray head, and honor the face of an old man, and you shall fear your God. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Stand up in the presence of the aged, show respect for the elderly, and revere your God. I am the Lord. Respect for elders is connected to reverence for God -- how you treat the vulnerable reveals your attitude toward the God who made them.

verses.text: “‘If a stranger lives as a foreigner with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong.
verses.explainer: When a foreigner resides among you in your land, do not mistreat them. Protection of immigrants and foreigners -- one of the most repeated commands in the Torah.

verses.text: The stranger who lives as a foreigner with you shall be to you as the native-born among you, and you shall love him as yourself; for you lived as foreigners in the land of Egypt. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: The foreigner residing among you must be treated as your native-born. Love them as yourself, for you were foreigners in Egypt. I am the Lord your God. 'Love them as yourself' -- the same command as verse 18, now explicitly extended to foreigners. Israel's own experience as immigrants in Egypt should produce empathy, not xenophobia.

thenVsNow.then: Israel's identity was forged in the experience of being foreigners and slaves in Egypt. God repeatedly connects this memory to ethical obligations: because you know what it is like to be the outsider, you must protect outsiders.
thenVsNow.now: Immigration policy remains one of the most contentious issues in modern politics. This verse insists that the ethical obligation to the foreigner is not secondary but is expressed in the same language as the obligation to the neighbor.

verses.text: “‘You shall do no unrighteousness in judgment, in measures of length, of weight, or of quantity.
verses.explainer: Do not use dishonest standards when measuring length, weight, or quantity. Economic fraud is a holiness violation.

verses.text: You shall have just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin. I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Use honest scales and honest weights, an honest ephah and an honest hin. I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt. The Exodus is invoked again -- God who liberated you from injustice demands that you practice justice.

verses.text: “‘You shall observe all my statutes, and all my ordinances, and do them. I am Yahweh.’”
verses.explainer: Keep all my decrees and all my laws and follow them. I am the Lord. The chapter ends as it began: with God's identity as the foundation for all ethical behavior.

explainer.summary: Chapter 20 prescribes penalties for the violations listed in chapters 18-19: death for sacrificing children to Molek, consulting mediums, cursing parents, adultery, and various sexual violations. 'Being cut off' is prescribed for other offenses. The chapter frames these penalties within the holiness framework: God has set Israel apart from the nations, and these punishments protect the community's distinctiveness.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 20 provides the enforcement mechanism for chapters 18-19. The laws without penalties are aspirations; with penalties, they are enforceable standards. The chapter shows that holiness is not optional but obligatory.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the penalties for various violations.

verses.text: “Moreover, you shall tell the children of Israel, ‘Anyone of the children of Israel, or of the strangers who live as foreigners in Israel, who gives any of his offspring to Molech; he shall surely be put to death. The people of the land shall stone him with stones.
verses.explainer: Any Israelite or foreigner who sacrifices any of his children to Molek is to be put to death. The members of the community are to stone him. Child sacrifice carries the death penalty without exception.

thenVsNow.then: Community stoning was not mob violence but a form of collective justice. The entire community participated in the execution, signifying that the crime offended the whole community and that responsibility for the sentence was shared.
thenVsNow.now: Modern legal systems have moved away from community execution toward state-administered justice. The principle that the most serious offenses against the vulnerable warrant the most severe penalties, however, continues in the form of enhanced sentences for crimes against children.

verses.text: I also will set my face against that person, and will cut him off from among his people because he has given of his offspring to Molech, to defile my sanctuary, and to profane my holy name.
verses.explainer: I myself will set my face against him and cut him off from his people because he has given his children to Molek, defiling my sanctuary and profaning my holy name. Even if the community fails to act, God himself will enforce the penalty. Human judicial failure does not mean the crime goes unaddressed.

verses.text: If the people of the land all hide their eyes from that person, when he gives of his offspring to Molech, and don’t put him to death;
verses.explainer: If the members of the community close their eyes when that man sacrifices one of his children to Molek and if they fail to put him to death --

verses.text: then I will set my face against that man, and against his family, and will cut him off, and all who play the prostitute after him, to play the prostitute with Molech, from among their people.
verses.explainer: I myself will set my face against him and his family and will cut off from their people both him and all who follow him in prostituting themselves to Molek. Communal complicity in evil extends God's judgment to those who knew and did nothing.

verses.text: “‘The person that turns to those who are mediums, and to the wizards, to play the prostitute after them, I will even set my face against that person, and will cut him off from among his people.
verses.explainer: I will set my face against anyone who turns to mediums and spiritists to prostitute themselves by following them, and I will cut them off from their people. Consulting spirits is treated as spiritual infidelity -- 'prostitution' against God.

verses.text: “‘Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be holy; for I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Consecrate yourselves and be holy, because I am the Lord your God. The holiness command is restated as the motivation for all the penalties that follow.

verses.text: You shall keep my statutes, and do them. I am Yahweh who sanctifies you.
verses.explainer: Keep my decrees and follow them. I am the Lord, who makes you holy. God is the active agent of holiness -- 'I make you holy.' The people obey, but God does the transforming.

verses.text: “‘For everyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death: he has cursed his father or his mother; his blood shall be upon him.
verses.explainer: Anyone who curses their father or mother is to be put to death. Because they have cursed their father or mother, their blood will be on their own head. Cursing parents strikes at the foundation of social order. The phrase 'their blood is on their own head' means they brought the consequence on themselves.

verses.text: “‘The man who commits adultery with another man’s wife, even he who commits adultery with his neighbor’s wife, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: If a man commits adultery with another man's wife -- with the wife of his neighbor -- both the adulterer and the adulteress are to be put to death. Both parties are held equally responsible.

verses.text: “‘The man who lies with his father’s wife has uncovered his father’s nakedness: both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with his father's wife, he has dishonored his father. Both the man and the woman are to be put to death.

verses.text: “‘If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death: they have committed a perversion; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with his daughter-in-law, both are to be put to death. What they have done is a perversion.

verses.text: “‘If a man lies with a male, as with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination: they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with a man as one does with a woman, both of them have done what is detestable. They are to be put to death.

verses.text: “‘If a man takes a wife and her mother, it is wickedness: they shall be burned with fire, both he and they; that there may be no wickedness among you.
verses.explainer: If a man marries both a woman and her mother, it is wicked. All three are to be burned in fire. The severity of the penalty reflects the severity of the violation.

verses.text: “‘If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death; and you shall kill the animal.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with an animal, he is to be put to death, and you must kill the animal.

verses.text: “‘If a woman approaches any animal, and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal. They shall surely be put to death. Their blood shall be upon them.
verses.explainer: If a woman approaches an animal to have sexual relations with it, kill both the woman and the animal.

verses.text: “‘If a man takes his sister, his father’s daughter, or his mother’s daughter, and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness; it is a shameful thing. They shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has uncovered his sister’s nakedness. He shall bear his iniquity.
verses.explainer: If a man marries his sister, the daughter of either his father or his mother, and they have sexual relations, it is a disgrace. They are to be publicly removed from their people.

verses.text: “‘If a man lies with a woman having her monthly period, and uncovers her nakedness; he has made naked her fountain, and she has uncovered the fountain of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with a woman during her monthly period, he has exposed the source of her flow and she has uncovered it. Both of them are to be cut off from their people.

verses.text: “‘You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother’s sister, nor of your father’s sister; for he has made his close relative naked. They shall bear their iniquity.
verses.explainer: Do not have sexual relations with the sister of either your mother or your father, for that would dishonor a close relative. Both of them would be held responsible.

verses.text: If a man lies with his uncle’s wife, he has uncovered his uncle’s nakedness. They shall bear their sin. They shall die childless.
verses.explainer: If a man has sexual relations with his aunt, he has dishonored his uncle. They will be held responsible and will die childless.

verses.text: “‘If a man takes his brother’s wife, it is an impurity. He has uncovered his brother’s nakedness. They shall be childless.
verses.explainer: If a man marries his brother's wife, it is an act of impurity. He has dishonored his brother. They will be childless.

verses.text: “‘You shall therefore keep all my statutes, and all my ordinances, and do them; that the land, where I am bringing you to dwell, may not vomit you out.
verses.explainer: Keep all my decrees and laws and follow them, so that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out. The land-vomiting metaphor from chapter 18 returns -- the land itself rejects covenant-breakers.

verses.text: You shall not walk in the customs of the nation, which I am casting out before you: for they did all these things, and therefore I abhorred them.
verses.explainer: You must not live according to the customs of the nations I am going to drive out before you. Because they did all these things, I abhorred them.

verses.text: But I have said to you, “You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess it, a land flowing with milk and honey.” I am Yahweh your God, who has separated you from the peoples.
verses.explainer: But I said to you: You will possess their land; I will give it to you as an inheritance, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the Lord your God, who has set you apart from the nations. God's gift of the land is conditional on maintaining holiness.

verses.text: “‘You shall therefore make a distinction between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean fowl and the clean: and you shall not make yourselves abominable by animal, or by bird, or by anything with which the ground teems, which I have separated from you as unclean for you.
verses.explainer: You must therefore make a distinction between clean and unclean animals and between unclean and clean birds. Do not defile yourselves by any animal or bird or anything that moves along the ground -- those that I have set apart as unclean for you. The dietary laws are connected to the holiness framework -- maintaining food distinctions reinforces Israel's distinctiveness.

verses.text: You shall be holy to me; for I, Yahweh, am holy, and have set you apart from the peoples, that you should be mine.
verses.explainer: You are to be holy to me because I, the Lord, am holy, and I have set you apart from the nations to be my own. The chapter's climactic verse: holiness is the purpose of Israel's election. God chose them not for privilege but for distinctiveness.

thenVsNow.then: Israel's 'chosenness' was not about superiority but about responsibility. Being set apart meant bearing the burden of representing God's character to the world.
thenVsNow.now: The concept of 'chosen people' continues to generate both pride and controversy. The biblical emphasis is consistently on obligation rather than privilege -- being chosen means being held to a higher standard.

verses.text: “‘A man or a woman that is a medium, or is a wizard, shall surely be put to death: they shall stone them with stones. Their blood shall be upon them.’”
verses.explainer: A man or woman who is a medium or spiritist among you must be put to death. You are to stone them; their blood will be on their own heads. The chapter ends where it began -- with the death penalty for occult practices, forming an inclusio (literary bracket).

explainer.summary: Priests are held to stricter holiness standards than ordinary Israelites. They may only mourn for their closest relatives. They must not shave their heads, clip their beards, or cut their bodies. The high priest may not mourn at all -- not even for his parents. Priests with physical defects may eat the sacred food but may not serve at the altar. These regulations are not about human worth but about the symbolic perfection required of those who represent God's holiness.
explainer.contextInBook: After the general holiness commands for all Israel (chapters 19-20), Leviticus now addresses the priests specifically. The logic is: if all Israel must be holy, the priests who mediate Israel's relationship with God must be even more so.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses, “Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them, ‘A priest shall not defile himself for the dead among his people;
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Moses to speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron. A priest must not make himself ceremonially unclean for any of his people who die. The priestly calling restricts even grief -- dead bodies cause the most severe contamination, and priests must minimize their contact.

verses.text: except for his relatives that are near to him: for his mother, for his father, for his son, for his daughter, for his brother,
verses.explainer: Except for a close relative -- his mother, father, son, daughter, brother.

verses.text: and for his virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband; for her he may defile himself.
verses.explainer: Or an unmarried sister who is dependent on him since she has no husband -- for her he may make himself unclean. Only the closest family members justify the priest's ceremonial contamination.

verses.text: He shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people, to profane himself.
verses.explainer: He must not make himself unclean for people related to him by marriage, and so defile himself. In-laws do not qualify for the mourning exception.

verses.text: “‘They shall not shave their heads or shave off the corners of their beards or make any cuttings in their flesh.
verses.explainer: Priests must not shave their heads or shave off the edges of their beards or cut their bodies. These grooming prohibitions prevent priests from participating in pagan mourning practices.

verses.text: They shall be holy to their God, and not profane the name of their God; for they offer the offerings of Yahweh made by fire, the bread of their God; therefore they shall be holy.
verses.explainer: They must be holy to their God and must not profane the name of their God. Because they present the food offerings to the Lord, they must be holy. Their daily service at God's altar requires continuous holiness.

verses.text: “‘They shall not marry a woman who is a prostitute, or profane. They shall not marry a woman divorced from her husband; for he is holy to his God.
verses.explainer: They must not marry women defiled by prostitution or divorced from their husbands, because priests are holy to their God. Marriage restrictions for priests are stricter than for other Israelites.

verses.text: You shall sanctify him therefore; for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you; for I Yahweh, who sanctify you, am holy.
verses.explainer: Regard them as holy because they offer up the food of your God. Consider them holy because I the Lord am holy -- I who make you holy.

verses.text: “‘The daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by playing the prostitute, she profanes her father. She shall be burned with fire.
verses.explainer: If a priest's daughter defiles herself by becoming a prostitute, she disgraces her father. She must be burned in the fire. The severity reflects the priest's family's heightened connection to holiness.

verses.text: “‘He who is the high priest among his brothers, upon whose head the anointing oil is poured, and that is consecrated to put on the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose, or tear his clothes.
verses.explainer: The high priest, the one among his brothers who has had the anointing oil poured on his head and who has been ordained to wear the priestly garments, must not let his hair become unkempt or tear his clothes. The high priest has the strictest restrictions of all.

verses.text: He must not go in to any dead body, or defile himself for his father, or for his mother.
verses.explainer: He must not enter a place where there is a dead body. He must not make himself unclean, even for his father or mother. The high priest cannot mourn even for his parents -- his consecration to God takes absolute precedence over family obligation.

thenVsNow.then: This was the most extreme expression of priestly dedication: the high priest's commitment to God's sanctuary overrode even the most natural human obligations, including mourning his own parents.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between vocational calling and family obligation continues to challenge religious leaders. The extreme nature of the high priestly restrictions illustrates that spiritual leadership sometimes requires extraordinary personal sacrifice.

verses.text: He shall not go out of the sanctuary, nor profane the sanctuary of his God; for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: He must not leave the sanctuary of his God or desecrate it, because he has been dedicated by the anointing oil of his God. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “‘He shall take a wife in her virginity.
verses.explainer: The woman he marries must be a virgin.

verses.text: A widow, or one divorced, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute, these he shall not marry: but a virgin of his own people shall he take as a wife.
verses.explainer: He must not marry a widow, a divorced woman, or a woman defiled by prostitution, but only a virgin from his own people. The high priest's marriage restrictions are stricter than ordinary priests' restrictions.

verses.text: He shall not profane his offspring among his people, for I am Yahweh who sanctifies him.’”
verses.explainer: So he will not defile his offspring among his people. I am the Lord, who makes him holy. The high priestly line must be maintained in purity for future generations.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about physical requirements for priestly service.

verses.text: “Say to Aaron, ‘None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a defect may approach to offer the bread of his God.
verses.explainer: For the generations to come, none of your descendants who has a defect may come near to offer the food of his God.

verses.text: For whatever man he is that has a defect, he shall not draw near: a blind man, or a lame, or he who has a flat nose, or any deformity,
verses.explainer: No man who has any defect may come near: no man who is blind or lame, disfigured or deformed.

verses.text: or a man who has an injured foot, or an injured hand,
verses.explainer: No man with a crippled foot or hand.

verses.text: or hunchbacked, or a dwarf, or one who has a defect in his eye, or an itching disease, or scabs, or who has damaged testicles.
verses.explainer: Or who is a hunchback or a dwarf, or who has any eye defect, or who has festering or running sores or damaged testicles.

verses.text: No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a defect shall come near to offer the offerings of Yahweh made by fire. Since he has a defect, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God.
verses.explainer: No descendant of Aaron the priest who has any defect is to come near to present the food offerings to the Lord. He has a defect; he must not come near to offer the food of his God.

verses.text: He shall eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy, and of the holy.
verses.explainer: He may eat the most holy food of his God, as well as the holy food. The person with a defect is not excluded from the priestly family or from eating sacred food. The restriction is only on altar service -- the visible, representative role.

thenVsNow.then: These requirements reflected the symbolic principle that what represents God must be 'whole' or 'complete.' The animals offered in sacrifice had to be without blemish, and so did the priest who offered them. This was about symbolic representation, not human value.
thenVsNow.now: Modern disability rights advocates rightly challenge any application of these standards to contemporary ministry. The New Testament moves away from physical criteria for service, emphasizing spiritual gifts and character instead (1 Timothy 3, Titus 1).

verses.text: He shall not come near to the veil, nor come near to the altar, because he has a defect; that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am Yahweh who sanctifies them.’”
verses.explainer: Yet because of his defect, he must not go near the curtain or approach the altar, and so desecrate these holy places. I am the Lord, who makes them holy.

verses.text: So Moses spoke to Aaron, and to his sons, and to all the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: So Moses told this to Aaron and his sons and to all the Israelites.

explainer.summary: This chapter regulates who may eat sacred offerings (only ritually clean priests and their households) and what may be offered (only unblemished animals). A priest who is unclean must not eat sacred food until he is purified. Non-priests may not eat sacred food except for certain household members. Sacrificial animals must be without defect -- no blind, injured, maimed, or diseased animals. The chapter protects the integrity of both the priesthood and the sacrificial system.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 22 continues the priestly regulations of chapter 21, extending from who may serve at the altar to what may be placed on it. Together, these chapters ensure that every dimension of the sacrificial system reflects God's holiness.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about treating sacred offerings with respect.

verses.text: “Tell Aaron and his sons to separate themselves from the holy things of the children of Israel, which they make holy to me, and that they not profane my holy name. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Tell Aaron and his sons to treat with respect the sacred offerings the Israelites consecrate to me, so they will not profane my holy name. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “Tell them, ‘If anyone of all your offspring throughout your generations approaches the holy things, which the children of Israel make holy to Yahweh, having his uncleanness on him, that soul shall be cut off from before me. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: If any of your descendants is ceremonially unclean and yet comes near the sacred offerings that the Israelites consecrate to the Lord, that person must be cut off from my presence. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “‘Whoever of the offspring of Aaron is a leper or has an issue; he shall not eat of the holy things, until he is clean. Whoever touches anything that is unclean by the dead, or a man whose offspring goes from him;
verses.explainer: If a descendant of Aaron has a defiling skin disease or a bodily discharge, he may not eat the sacred offerings until he is cleansed. He will also be unclean if he touches something defiled by a corpse or by anyone who has an emission of semen.

verses.text: or whoever touches any creeping thing, whereby he may be made unclean, or a man of whom he may take uncleanness, whatever uncleanness he has;
verses.explainer: Or if he touches any crawling thing that makes him unclean, or any person who makes him unclean, whatever the uncleanness may be.

verses.text: the person that touches any such shall be unclean until the evening, and shall not eat of the holy things, unless he bathes his body in water.
verses.explainer: The one who touches any such thing will be unclean till evening. He must not eat any of the sacred offerings unless he has bathed.

verses.text: When the sun is down, he shall be clean; and afterward he shall eat of the holy things, because it is his bread.
verses.explainer: When the sun goes down, he will be clean, and after that he may eat the sacred offerings, for they are his food.

verses.text: That which dies of itself, or is torn by animals, he shall not eat, defiling himself by it. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: He must not eat anything found dead or torn by wild animals, and so become unclean. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “‘They shall therefore follow my commandment, lest they bear sin for it, and die therein, if they profane it. I am Yahweh who sanctifies them.
verses.explainer: The priests are to perform my service in such a way that they do not become guilty and die for treating it with contempt. I am the Lord, who makes them holy.

verses.text: “‘No stranger shall eat of the holy thing: a foreigner living with the priests, or a hired servant, shall not eat of the holy thing.
verses.explainer: No one outside a priest's family may eat the sacred offering, nor may the guest of a priest or his hired worker eat it. Sacred food is restricted to priestly households.

verses.text: But if a priest buys a slave, purchased by his money, he shall eat of it; and such as are born in his house, they shall eat of his bread.
verses.explainer: But if a priest buys a slave with money, or if slaves are born in his household, they may eat his food. Household members, including slaves, are considered part of the priestly family for purposes of sacred food.

verses.text: If a priest’s daughter is married to an outsider, she shall not eat of the heave offering of the holy things.
verses.explainer: If a priest's daughter marries anyone other than a priest, she may not eat any of the sacred contributions. Marrying outside the priesthood means leaving the priestly household's food privileges.

verses.text: But if a priest’s daughter is a widow, or divorced, and has no child, and has returned to her father’s house, as in her youth, she may eat of her father’s bread: but no stranger shall eat any of it.
verses.explainer: But if a priest's daughter becomes a widow or is divorced, yet has no children, and she returns to live in her father's household as in her youth, she may eat her father's food. Returning to the priestly household restores the food privilege.

verses.text: “‘If a man eats something holy unwittingly, then he shall add the fifth part of its value to it, and shall give the holy thing to the priest.
verses.explainer: Anyone who eats a sacred offering by mistake must make restitution to the priest for the offering and add a fifth of the value. The familiar twenty-percent surcharge for sacred violations.

verses.text: The priests shall not profane the holy things of the children of Israel, which they offer to Yahweh,
verses.explainer: The priests must not desecrate the sacred offerings the Israelites present to the Lord.

verses.text: and so cause them to bear the iniquity that brings guilt, when they eat their holy things; for I am Yahweh who sanctifies them.’”
verses.explainer: By allowing them to eat the sacred offerings, the priests would bring upon them guilt requiring payment. I am the Lord, who makes them holy.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the quality of animals that may be sacrificed.

verses.text: “Speak to Aaron, and to his sons, and to all the children of Israel, and say to them, ‘Whoever is of the house of Israel, or of the foreigners in Israel, who offers his offering, whether it is any of their vows, or any of their freewill offerings, which they offer to Yahweh for a burnt offering;
verses.explainer: When any Israelite or foreigner presents a gift for a burnt offering to the Lord, whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering.

verses.text: that you may be accepted, you shall offer a male without defect, of the bulls, of the sheep, or of the goats.
verses.explainer: You must present a male without defect from the cattle, sheep, or goats in order for it to be accepted on your behalf. The same 'without defect' standard established in chapter 1.

verses.text: But whatever has a defect, that you shall not offer; for it shall not be acceptable for you.
verses.explainer: Do not bring anything with a defect, because it will not be accepted on your behalf.

verses.text: Whoever offers a sacrifice of peace offerings to Yahweh to accomplish a vow, or for a freewill offering, of the herd or of the flock, it shall be perfect to be accepted. It shall have no defect.
verses.explainer: When anyone brings a peace offering to the Lord to fulfill a special vow or as a freewill offering, it must be without defect or blemish to be accepted.

verses.text: Blind, injured, maimed, having a wart, festering, or having a running sore: you shall not offer these to Yahweh, nor make an offering by fire of them on the altar to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Do not offer to the Lord the blind, the injured, the maimed, or anything with warts or festering or running sores. Do not place any of these on the altar as a food offering.

verses.text: Either a bull or a lamb that has any deformity or lacking in his parts, that you may offer for a freewill offering; but for a vow it shall not be accepted.
verses.explainer: You may present as a freewill offering an ox or sheep that is deformed or stunted, but it will not be accepted in fulfillment of a vow. A lower-stakes freewill offering has slightly more flexibility than a vow offering.

verses.text: You must not offer to Yahweh that which has its testicles bruised, crushed, broken, or cut. You must not do this in your land.
verses.explainer: You must not offer to the Lord an animal whose testicles are bruised, crushed, torn, or cut. You must not do this in your own land.

verses.text: You must not offer the bread of your God from the hand of a foreigner as any of these; because their corruption is in them. There is a defect in them. They shall not be accepted for you.’”
verses.explainer: And you must not accept such animals from the hand of a foreigner and offer them as the food of your God. They will not be accepted on your behalf because they are deformed and have defects.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the age requirements for sacrificial animals.

verses.text: “When a bull, or a sheep, or a goat, is born, then it shall remain seven days with its mother; and from the eighth day and thenceforth it shall be accepted for the offering of an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: When a calf, a lamb, or a goat is born, it is to remain with its mother for seven days. From the eighth day on, it will be acceptable as a food offering to the Lord. The eighth-day principle again -- new life must mature before it can be offered.

thenVsNow.then: The seven-day waiting period may have served both practical and symbolic purposes: practically, it ensured the animal was viable; symbolically, it allowed the animal to complete one full week of life before consecration.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of not rushing to sacrifice -- allowing proper time for maturity -- applies broadly to spiritual life. Premature commitments and hasty dedications may not reflect genuine readiness.

verses.text: Whether it is a cow or ewe, you shall not kill it and its young both in one day.
verses.explainer: Do not slaughter a cow or a sheep and its young on the same day. This compassionate regulation prevents the cruelty of killing a mother and offspring on the same day. Even in the sacrificial system, unnecessary cruelty is forbidden.

verses.text: “When you sacrifice a sacrifice of thanksgiving to Yahweh, you shall sacrifice it so that you may be accepted.
verses.explainer: When you sacrifice a thank offering to the Lord, sacrifice it in such a way that it will be accepted on your behalf.

verses.text: It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until the morning. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: It must be eaten that same day; leave none of it till morning. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “Therefore you shall keep my commandments, and do them. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Keep my commands and follow them. I am the Lord.

verses.text: You shall not profane my holy name, but I will be made holy among the children of Israel. I am Yahweh who makes you holy,
verses.explainer: Do not profane my holy name, for I must be acknowledged as holy by the Israelites. I am the Lord, who made you holy.

verses.text: who brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God. I am Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: And who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the Lord. The Exodus reference closes the section, reminding Israel that their obligation to holiness flows from their rescue from slavery.

explainer.summary: God establishes Israel's sacred calendar -- the annual rhythm of festivals that structure the nation's worship. The chapter covers the Sabbath (weekly), Passover and Unleavened Bread (spring), Firstfruits (spring), the Festival of Weeks/Pentecost (early summer), the Festival of Trumpets (fall), the Day of Atonement (fall), and the Festival of Tabernacles/Booths (fall). Each festival connects Israel to its history, its agricultural life, and its relationship with God. Together they create a year-long cycle of remembrance, gratitude, repentance, and celebration.
themes: Sacred time -- festivals as encounters with God
explainer.contextInBook: After holiness in personal conduct (chapters 19-20) and priestly service (chapters 21-22), Leviticus addresses holiness in time. The festivals sanctify the calendar itself, ensuring that Israel's year is structured around God rather than around purely economic or social concerns.

wordStudies.translatedAs: An appointed time or meeting
wordStudies.meaning: An appointed time or meeting. The festivals are not arbitrary holidays but divine appointments -- times when God has scheduled an encounter with his people. The word implies that God initiates the meeting and Israel shows up.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A holy convocation or calling-together
wordStudies.meaning: A holy convocation or calling-together. The community is summoned to gather. These were not optional events but obligatory assemblies where the entire community participated.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the appointed festivals.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘The set feasts of Yahweh, which you shall proclaim to be holy convocations, even these are my set feasts.
verses.explainer: These are my appointed festivals, the appointed festivals of the Lord, which you are to proclaim as sacred assemblies. God calls them 'my' festivals -- they belong to him, not to Israel.

verses.text: “‘Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day is a Sabbath of solemn rest, a holy convocation; you shall do no kind of work. It is a Sabbath to Yahweh in all your dwellings.
verses.explainer: There are six days when you may work, but the seventh day is a day of sabbath rest, a day of sacred assembly. You are not to do any work; wherever you live, it is a sabbath to the Lord. The Sabbath is listed first as the foundational festival. It comes every week, structuring all of life around a rhythm of work and rest.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, where survival depended on constant labor, a mandatory day of rest was revolutionary. No other culture commanded an entire nation to stop working every seventh day. It was an act of trust that God would provide.
thenVsNow.now: The concept of a regular day of rest has shaped labor law worldwide. The 'weekend' is a secularized version of the Sabbath principle. The tension between productivity and rest remains one of the central challenges of modern life.

verses.text: “‘These are the set feasts of Yahweh, even holy convocations, which you shall proclaim in their appointed season.
verses.explainer: These are the Lord's appointed festivals, the sacred assemblies you are to proclaim at their appointed times. Now the annual festivals begin.

verses.text: In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month in the evening, is Yahweh’s Passover.
verses.explainer: The Lord's Passover begins at twilight on the fourteenth day of the first month. Passover commemorates God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt -- the foundational event of Israel's identity.

verses.text: On the fifteenth day of the same month is the feast of unleavened bread to Yahweh. Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.
verses.explainer: On the fifteenth day of that month the Lord's Festival of Unleavened Bread begins. For seven days you must eat bread made without yeast. Seven days without leaven -- the bread of haste, reminding Israel that they left Egypt so quickly there was no time for dough to rise.

verses.text: In the first day you shall have a holy convocation. You shall do no regular work.
verses.explainer: On the first day hold a sacred assembly and do no regular work.

verses.text: But you shall offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh seven days. In the seventh day is a holy convocation: you shall do no regular work.’”
verses.explainer: For seven days present a food offering to the Lord. On the seventh day hold a sacred assembly and do no regular work.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the Firstfruits offering.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘When you have come into the land which I give to you, and shall reap its harvest, then you shall bring the sheaf of the first fruits of your harvest to the priest:
verses.explainer: When you enter the land I am going to give you and you reap its harvest, bring to the priest a sheaf of the first grain you harvest. Firstfruits acknowledges that the harvest belongs to God. You give the first portion before taking any for yourself.

verses.text: and he shall wave the sheaf before Yahweh, to be accepted for you. On the next day after the Sabbath the priest shall wave it.
verses.explainer: He is to wave the sheaf before the Lord so it will be accepted on your behalf. The priest is to wave it on the day after the Sabbath.

verses.text: On the day when you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a male lamb without defect a year old for a burnt offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: On the day you wave the sheaf, you must sacrifice a year-old lamb without defect as a burnt offering to the Lord.

verses.text: The meal offering with it shall be two tenths of an ephah of fine flour mingled with oil, an offering made by fire to Yahweh for a pleasant aroma; and the drink offering with it shall be of wine, the fourth part of a hin.
verses.explainer: Together with its grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with olive oil -- a food offering with a pleasing aroma -- and its drink offering of a quarter of a hin of wine.

verses.text: You must not eat bread, or roasted grain, or fresh grain, until this same day, until you have brought the offering of your God. This is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwellings.
verses.explainer: You must not eat any bread or roasted or new grain until the very day you bring this offering. This is a lasting ordinance for generations to come.

verses.text: “‘You shall count from the next day after the Sabbath, from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering; seven Sabbaths shall be completed:
verses.explainer: From the day after the Sabbath, the day you brought the sheaf of the wave offering, count off seven full weeks. The counting of fifty days begins the period between Firstfruits and the Festival of Weeks.

verses.text: even to the next day after the seventh Sabbath you shall count fifty days; and you shall offer a new meal offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Count off fifty days up to the day after the seventh Sabbath, and then present an offering of new grain to the Lord. Fifty days later -- Pentecost (from the Greek word for 'fiftieth'). This celebrates the wheat harvest.

verses.text: You shall bring out of your habitations two loaves of bread for a wave offering made of two tenths of an ephah of fine flour. They shall be baked with yeast, for first fruits to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: From wherever you live, bring two loaves made of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, baked with yeast, as a wave offering of firstfruits to the Lord. Uniquely, these loaves contain yeast -- the only time leavened bread is brought as a wave offering.

verses.text: You shall present with the bread seven lambs without defect a year old, one young bull, and two rams. They shall be a burnt offering to Yahweh, with their meal offering, and their drink offerings, even an offering made by fire, of a sweet aroma to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Present with this bread seven male lambs, each a year old and without defect, one young bull, and two rams. They will be a burnt offering to the Lord.

verses.text: You shall offer one male goat for a sin offering, and two male lambs a year old for a sacrifice of peace offerings.
verses.explainer: Then sacrifice one male goat for a sin offering and two lambs, each a year old, for a peace offering.

verses.text: The priest shall wave them with the bread of the first fruits for a wave offering before Yahweh, with the two lambs. They shall be holy to Yahweh for the priest.
verses.explainer: The priest is to wave the two lambs before the Lord as a wave offering, together with the bread of the firstfruits. They are a sacred offering to the Lord for the priest.

verses.text: You shall make proclamation on the same day: there shall be a holy convocation to you; you shall do no regular work. This is a statute forever in all your dwellings throughout your generations.
verses.explainer: On that same day you are to proclaim a sacred assembly and do no regular work. This is a lasting ordinance wherever you live.

verses.text: “‘When you reap the harvest of your land, you must not wholly reap into the corners of your field, and you must not gather the gleanings of your harvest. You must leave them for the poor, and for the foreigner. I am Yahweh your God.’”
verses.explainer: When you reap the harvest of your land, do not reap to the very edges of your field or gather the gleanings. Leave them for the poor and for the foreigner. I am the Lord your God. The gleaning law from 19:9-10 is repeated here -- even festival celebrations must include provision for the poor.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the Festival of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah in later tradition).

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, saying, ‘In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, shall be a solemn rest to you, a memorial of blowing of trumpets, a holy convocation.
verses.explainer: On the first day of the seventh month you are to have a day of sabbath rest, a sacred assembly commemorated with trumpet blasts. The trumpet blast announces the beginning of the most sacred month in Israel's calendar -- the month containing both the Day of Atonement and the Festival of Tabernacles.

thenVsNow.then: The trumpet blast (shofar, a ram's horn) served as a wake-up call for the nation to prepare for the Day of Atonement ten days later. The sound was meant to stir Israel to self-examination and repentance.
thenVsNow.now: In Judaism, this became Rosh Hashanah (New Year). The ten days between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are the 'Days of Awe' -- a period of intense self-examination and repentance that shapes Jewish spiritual practice to this day.

verses.text: You shall do no regular work; and you shall offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh.’”
verses.explainer: Do no regular work, but present a food offering to the Lord.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the Day of Atonement.

verses.text: “However on the tenth day of this seventh month is the day of atonement: it shall be a holy convocation to you, and you shall afflict yourselves; and you shall offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. Hold a sacred assembly and deny yourselves, and present a food offering to the Lord.

verses.text: You shall do no kind of work in that same day; for it is a day of atonement, to make atonement for you before Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not do any work on that day, because it is the Day of Atonement, when atonement is made for you before the Lord your God.

verses.text: For whoever it is who shall not deny himself in that same day; shall be cut off from his people.
verses.explainer: Those who do not deny themselves on that day must be cut off from their people. Failure to observe the Day of Atonement carries the 'cut off' penalty -- this is the most serious fast in Israel's calendar.

verses.text: Whoever it is who does any kind of work in that same day, that person I will destroy from among his people.
verses.explainer: I will destroy from among their people anyone who does any work on that day.

verses.text: You shall do no kind of work: it is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwellings.
verses.explainer: You shall do no work at all. This is a lasting ordinance wherever you live.

verses.text: It shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest for you, and you shall deny yourselves. In the ninth day of the month at evening, from evening to evening, you shall keep your Sabbath.”
verses.explainer: It is a day of sabbath rest for you, and you must deny yourselves. From the evening of the ninth day of the month until the following evening you are to observe your sabbath. Day of Atonement runs from sunset to sunset -- the biblical reckoning of a day.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the Festival of Tabernacles (Sukkot).

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and say, ‘On the fifteenth day of this seventh month is the feast of tents for seven days to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The fifteenth day of the seventh month the Lord's Festival of Tabernacles begins, and it lasts for seven days. The final major festival of the year -- a week-long celebration of harvest and remembrance of wilderness wandering.

thenVsNow.then: The Festival of Tabernacles (Sukkot) required Israelites to live in temporary shelters (booths) for seven days, remembering that their ancestors lived in temporary shelters during the forty years of wilderness wandering.
thenVsNow.now: Sukkot remains one of the most joyful holidays in Judaism. Families build and eat in temporary shelters, remembering both God's provision in the wilderness and the fragility of human life. Many Christians see Sukkot as foreshadowing God 'tabernacling' among humans in the incarnation (John 1:14).

verses.text: On the first day shall be a holy convocation: you shall do no regular work.
verses.explainer: The first day is a sacred assembly; do no regular work.

verses.text: Seven days you shall offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh. On the eighth day shall be a holy convocation to you; and you shall offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh. It is a solemn assembly; you shall do no regular work.
verses.explainer: For seven days present food offerings to the Lord, and on the eighth day hold a sacred assembly and present a food offering. It is the closing special assembly; do no regular work.

verses.text: “‘These are the appointed feasts of Yahweh, which you shall proclaim to be holy convocations, to offer an offering made by fire to Yahweh, a burnt offering, and a meal offering, a sacrifice, and drink offerings, each on its own day—
verses.explainer: These are the Lord's appointed festivals, which you are to proclaim as sacred assemblies for bringing food offerings to the Lord.

verses.text: besides the Sabbaths of Yahweh, and besides your gifts, and besides all your vows, and besides all your freewill offerings, which you give to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: These offerings are in addition to those for the Lord's Sabbaths and in addition to your gifts and whatever you have vowed and all the freewill offerings you give to the Lord.

verses.text: “‘So on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when you have gathered in the fruits of the land, you shall keep the feast of Yahweh seven days: on the first day shall be a solemn rest, and on the eighth day shall be a solemn rest.
verses.explainer: Beginning with the fifteenth day of the seventh month, after you have gathered the crops of the land, celebrate the festival to the Lord for seven days.

verses.text: You shall take on the first day the fruit of goodly trees, branches of palm trees, and boughs of thick trees, and willows of the brook; and you shall rejoice before Yahweh your God seven days.
verses.explainer: On the first day you are to take branches from luxuriant trees -- from palms, willows, and other leafy trees -- and rejoice before the Lord your God for seven days. The 'four species' (later tradition: palm, myrtle, willow, and citron) are waved in celebration.

verses.text: You shall keep it as a feast to Yahweh seven days in the year. It is a statute forever throughout your generations. You shall keep it in the seventh month.
verses.explainer: Celebrate this as a festival to the Lord for seven days each year. This is a lasting ordinance; celebrate it in the seventh month.

verses.text: You shall dwell in temporary shelters for seven days. All who are native-born in Israel shall dwell in temporary shelters,
verses.explainer: Live in temporary shelters for seven days. All native-born Israelites are to live in such shelters.

verses.text: that your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in temporary shelters, when I brought them out of the land of Egypt. I am Yahweh your God.’”
verses.explainer: So your descendants will know that I had the Israelites live in temporary shelters when I brought them out of Egypt. I am the Lord your God. The shelters are teaching tools -- they preserve the memory of dependence on God during the wilderness wandering.

verses.text: Moses declared to the children of Israel the appointed feasts of Yahweh.
verses.explainer: So Moses announced to the Israelites the appointed festivals of the Lord.

explainer.summary: This chapter has two parts: regulations for the Tabernacle lampstand and showbread, and a narrative about a man who blasphemed God's name. The lampstand must burn continually, and twelve loaves of bread must be set before the Lord every Sabbath. When a man of mixed Israelite-Egyptian heritage blasphemes during a fight, Moses consults God, who prescribes death by stoning and articulates the lex talionis ('eye for an eye') principle -- the same law for native and foreigner alike.
themes: Perpetual light and bread in God's presence
One law for native and foreigner
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter interrupts the legal sections with a narrative, grounding the laws in a real situation. The blasphemy case demonstrates that the holiness laws are not hypothetical but require actual enforcement.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the lampstand.

verses.text: “Command the children of Israel, that they bring to you pure olive oil beaten for the light, to cause a lamp to burn continually.
verses.explainer: Command the Israelites to bring you clear oil of pressed olives for the light so that the lamps may be kept burning continually. The lamps symbolize God's light shining perpetually in the darkness.

thenVsNow.then: The perpetual lamp in the Tabernacle represented God's ongoing presence and watchfulness. The light never went out -- God never sleeps.
thenVsNow.now: The 'eternal light' (ner tamid) remains a central feature of Jewish synagogues, burning continuously above the Torah ark. It symbolizes God's constant presence.

verses.text: Outside of the veil of the Testimony, in the Tent of Meeting, shall Aaron keep it in order from evening to morning before Yahweh continually: it shall be a statute forever throughout your generations.
verses.explainer: Outside the curtain that shields the ark of the covenant law in the Tent of Meeting, Aaron is to tend the lamps before the Lord from evening till morning, continually.

verses.text: He shall keep in order the lamps on the pure gold lamp stand before Yahweh continually.
verses.explainer: The lamps on the pure gold lampstand before the Lord must be tended continually.

verses.text: “You shall take fine flour, and bake twelve cakes of it: two tenths of an ephah shall be in one cake.
verses.explainer: Take the finest flour and bake twelve loaves of bread, using two-tenths of an ephah for each loaf.

verses.text: You shall set them in two rows, six on a row, on the pure gold table before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Arrange them in two stacks, six in each stack, on the table of pure gold before the Lord. Twelve loaves representing the twelve tribes -- the entire nation perpetually present before God.

verses.text: You shall put pure frankincense on each row, that it may be to the bread for a memorial, even an offering made by fire to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: By each stack put some pure incense as a memorial portion to represent the bread and to be a food offering presented to the Lord.

verses.text: Every Sabbath day he shall set it in order before Yahweh continually. It is an everlasting covenant on the behalf of the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: This bread is to be set out before the Lord regularly, Sabbath after Sabbath, on behalf of the Israelites, as a lasting covenant.

verses.text: It shall be for Aaron and his sons; and they shall eat it in a holy place: for it is most holy to him of the offerings of Yahweh made by fire by a perpetual statute.”
verses.explainer: It belongs to Aaron and his sons, who are to eat it in the sanctuary area, because it is a most holy part of their perpetual share of the food offerings to the Lord.

verses.text: The son of an Israelite woman, whose father was an Egyptian, went out among the children of Israel; and the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel strove together in the camp.
verses.explainer: Now the son of an Israelite mother and an Egyptian father went out among the Israelites. A fight broke out in the camp between him and an Israelite. The narrative breaks into the legal material with a real incident.
