verses.text: The son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the Name, and cursed; and they brought him to Moses. His mother’s name was Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan.
verses.explainer: The son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the Name with a curse. So they brought him to Moses. His mother's name was Shelomith, daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan. Blaspheming 'the Name' (ha-Shem) -- speaking God's name with contempt. The specific identification of his family shows this is a real historical incident.

verses.text: They put him in custody, until Yahweh’s will should be declared to them.
verses.explainer: They put him in custody until the will of the Lord should be made clear to them. Moses does not presume to know the answer. He consults God directly.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: Then the Lord speaks to Moses.

verses.text: “Bring out of the camp him who cursed; and let all who heard him lay their hands on his head, and let all the congregation stone him.
verses.explainer: Take the blasphemer outside the camp. All those who heard him are to lay their hands on his head, and the entire assembly is to stone him. The witnesses lay hands on the accused -- transferring the guilt back to him and confirming their testimony.

verses.text: You shall speak to the children of Israel, saying, ‘Whoever curses his God shall bear his sin.
verses.explainer: Say to the Israelites: Anyone who curses their God will be held responsible.

verses.text: He who blasphemes Yahweh’s name, he shall surely be put to death. All the congregation shall certainly stone him. The foreigner as well as the native-born, when he blasphemes the Name, shall be put to death.
verses.explainer: Anyone who blasphemes the name of the Lord is to be put to death. The entire assembly must stone them. Whether foreigner or native-born, when they blaspheme the Name they are to be put to death.

verses.text: “‘He who strikes any man mortally shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: Anyone who takes the life of a human being is to be put to death. Murder carries the death penalty.

verses.text: He who strikes an animal mortally shall make it good, life for life.
verses.explainer: Anyone who takes the life of someone's animal must make restitution -- life for life. Animal killing requires replacement, not death.

verses.text: If anyone injures his neighbor; as he has done, so shall it be done to him:
verses.explainer: Anyone who injures their neighbor is to be injured in the same manner.

verses.text: fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth; as he has injured someone, so shall it be done to him.
verses.explainer: Fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. The one who has inflicted the injury must suffer the same injury. The lex talionis -- often misunderstood as brutal, it was actually a limitation on revenge. You cannot escalate: the maximum penalty is what was done to you, nothing more.

thenVsNow.then: In a world where blood feuds could escalate endlessly -- you killed my brother, so I kill your entire family -- the 'eye for eye' principle was a restraint. It limited punishment to proportional justice and prevented revenge spirals.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus explicitly addressed this law in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:38-39), calling his followers to go beyond proportional justice to non-retaliation and enemy love. Modern legal systems have adopted the proportionality principle while moving from physical retaliation to fines and imprisonment.

verses.text: He who kills an animal shall make it good; and he who kills a man shall be put to death.
verses.explainer: Whoever kills an animal must make restitution, but whoever kills a human being is to be put to death. A clear distinction between the value of animal and human life.

verses.text: You shall have one kind of law for the foreigner as well as the native-born; for I am Yahweh your God.’”
verses.explainer: You are to have the same law for the foreigner and the native-born. I am the Lord your God. One legal standard for everyone -- no special treatment for citizens over foreigners.

thenVsNow.then: In most ancient legal systems, foreigners had fewer legal protections than citizens. Israel's insistence on equal justice for all was distinctive and countercultural.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of equal justice regardless of citizenship status remains an aspiration in modern legal systems, frequently debated in the context of immigration law and human rights.

verses.text: Moses spoke to the children of Israel; and they brought him who had cursed out of the camp, and stoned him with stones. The children of Israel did as Yahweh commanded Moses.
verses.explainer: Then Moses spoke to the Israelites, and they took the blasphemer outside the camp and stoned him. The Israelites did as the Lord commanded Moses. The narrative concludes with the sentence carried out -- the law is enforced, not just articulated.

explainer.summary: This extraordinary chapter extends the Sabbath principle from days to years. Every seventh year, the land rests (the sabbatical year). Every fiftieth year is the Jubilee -- a radical economic reset when slaves are freed, debts forgiven, and ancestral land returned to its original families. The theological foundation is that the land belongs to God, the Israelites are his tenants, and no permanent economic disparity is acceptable in God's community. It is the most radical economic legislation in the ancient world.
themes: The land belongs to God, not to humans
Sabbath rest extended to the land itself
Freedom from permanent slavery and debt
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 25 is the socioeconomic climax of the Holiness Code. Holiness is not just about worship, diet, and sexual ethics -- it demands an economic system that prevents permanent poverty and enslavement. The God who freed Israel from slavery will not tolerate slavery within Israel.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Probably from the ram's horn (yovel) blown to anno
wordStudies.meaning: Probably from the ram's horn (yovel) blown to announce the year. Every fiftieth year, property returned to original families, Israelite slaves were freed, and the economic system was reset. The word has entered English to mean any major anniversary or celebration.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The act of buying back something that was lost or
wordStudies.meaning: The act of buying back something that was lost or sold. A 'kinsman-redeemer' (go'el) was a relative who had the right and obligation to buy back family property or free a family member from slavery. The concept becomes central to Christian theology.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Moses in Mount Sinai,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses on Mount Sinai. The setting is emphasized -- these economic laws come directly from God at Sinai, carrying the same authority as the Ten Commandments.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and tell them, ‘When you come into the land which I give you, then the land shall keep a Sabbath to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: When you enter the land I am going to give you, the land itself must observe a sabbath to the Lord. The land gets a Sabbath -- even the earth needs rest.

thenVsNow.then: Letting farmland lie fallow every seventh year was agriculturally sound -- it allowed the soil to regenerate. But the theological point was deeper: the land belongs to God, and he decides its schedule.
thenVsNow.now: Modern agriculture increasingly recognizes the importance of letting soil rest and regenerate. The sabbatical year anticipated sustainable farming practices by millennia.

verses.text: You shall sow your field six years, and you shall prune your vineyard six years, and gather in its fruits;
verses.explainer: For six years sow your fields, and for six years prune your vineyards and gather their crops.

verses.text: but in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest for the land, a Sabbath to Yahweh. You shall not sow your field or prune your vineyard.
verses.explainer: But in the seventh year the land is to have a year of sabbath rest, a sabbath to the Lord. Do not sow your fields or prune your vineyards.

verses.text: What grows of itself in your harvest you shall not reap, and you shall not gather the grapes of your undressed vine. It shall be a year of solemn rest for the land.
verses.explainer: Do not reap what grows of itself or harvest the untended vines. The land is to have a year of rest.

verses.text: The Sabbath of the land shall be for food for you; for yourself, for your servant, for your maid, for your hired servant, and for your stranger, who lives as a foreigner with you.
verses.explainer: Whatever the land yields during the sabbath year will be food for you -- for yourself, your male and female servants, and the hired worker and temporary resident who live among you.

verses.text: For your livestock also, and for the animals that are in your land, shall all its increase be for food.
verses.explainer: As well as for your livestock and the wild animals in your land. Whatever the land produces may be eaten. Even animals benefit from the sabbatical -- the entire created order shares in the rest.

verses.text: “‘You shall count off seven Sabbaths of years, seven times seven years; and there shall be to you the days of seven Sabbaths of years, even forty-nine years.
verses.explainer: Count off seven sabbath years -- seven times seven years -- so that the seven sabbath years amount to a period of forty-nine years. Seven sevens: the ultimate Sabbath cycle.

verses.text: Then you shall sound the loud trumpet on the tenth day of the seventh month. On the Day of Atonement you shall sound the trumpet throughout all your land.
verses.explainer: Then have the trumpet sounded everywhere on the tenth day of the seventh month; on the Day of Atonement sound the trumpet throughout your land. The Jubilee begins on the Day of Atonement -- spiritual atonement and economic liberation are linked.

verses.text: You shall make the fiftieth year holy, and proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants. It shall be a jubilee to you; and each of you shall return to his own property, and each of you shall return to his family.
verses.explainer: Consecrate the fiftieth year and proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants. It shall be a jubilee for you; each of you is to return to your family property and to your own clan. This verse is inscribed on the Liberty Bell in Philadelphia. It is the most radical economic legislation in the ancient world -- a complete reset of the social order every fifty years.

verses.text: That fiftieth year shall be a jubilee to you. In it you shall not sow, neither reap that which grows of itself, nor gather from the undressed vines.
verses.explainer: The fiftieth year shall be a jubilee for you; do not sow and do not reap what grows of itself or harvest the untended vines.

verses.text: For it is a jubilee; it shall be holy to you. You shall eat of its increase out of the field.
verses.explainer: For it is a jubilee and is to be holy for you; eat only what is taken directly from the fields.

verses.text: “‘In this Year of Jubilee each of you shall return to his property.
verses.explainer: In this Year of Jubilee everyone is to return to their own property. All land reverts to its original family.

verses.text: “‘If you sell anything to your neighbor, or buy from your neighbor, you shall not wrong one another.
verses.explainer: If you sell land to any of your own people or buy land from them, do not take advantage of each other. Fair dealing even in real estate.

verses.text: According to the number of years after the Jubilee you shall buy from your neighbor. According to the number of years of the crops he shall sell to you.
verses.explainer: You are to buy from your own people on the basis of the number of years since the Jubilee. And they are to sell to you on the basis of the number of years left for harvesting crops. Land sales are really long-term leases priced by remaining productive years.

verses.text: According to the length of the years you shall increase its price, and according to the shortness of the years you shall diminish its price; for he is selling the number of the crops to you.
verses.explainer: When the years are many, you are to increase the price, and when the years are few, you are to decrease the price, because what is really being sold to you is the number of crops. The price is based on productivity, not speculation.

verses.text: You shall not wrong one another; but you shall fear your God: for I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not take advantage of each other, but fear your God. I am the Lord your God.

verses.text: “‘Therefore you shall do my statutes, and keep my ordinances and do them; and you shall dwell in the land in safety.
verses.explainer: Follow my decrees and be careful to obey my laws, and you will live safely in the land.

verses.text: The land shall yield its fruit, and you shall eat your fill, and dwell therein in safety.
verses.explainer: Then the land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and live there in safety.

verses.text: If you said, “What shall we eat the seventh year? Behold, we shall not sow, nor gather in our increase”;
verses.explainer: You may ask, 'What will we eat in the seventh year if we do not plant or harvest our crops?' God anticipates the practical objection.

verses.text: then I will command my blessing on you in the sixth year, and it shall bear fruit for the three years.
verses.explainer: I will send you such a blessing in the sixth year that the land will yield enough for three years. God promises a bumper crop in the sixth year to cover the sabbatical.

verses.text: You shall sow the eighth year, and eat of the fruits, the old store; until the ninth year, until its fruits come in, you shall eat the old store.
verses.explainer: While you plant during the eighth year, you will eat from the old crop and will continue to eat from it until the harvest of the ninth year comes in. The provision covers the gap completely.

verses.text: “‘The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine; for you are strangers and live as foreigners with me.
verses.explainer: The land must not be sold permanently, because the land is mine and you reside in my land as foreigners and strangers. This is the theological foundation: God owns the land. Israel is a tenant, not an owner. No one can permanently accumulate land at others' expense because no one truly owns it.

thenVsNow.then: In a society where land was the primary source of wealth and security, declaring that no one ultimately owns the land was revolutionary. It prevented the formation of a permanent landed aristocracy.
thenVsNow.now: Land reform remains one of the most contentious political issues worldwide. The Jubilee principle -- that excessive concentration of resources violates divine intent -- has influenced social reformers, liberation theologians, and economic ethicists across centuries.

verses.text: In all the land of your possession you shall grant a redemption for the land.
verses.explainer: Throughout the land that you hold as a possession, you must provide for the redemption of the land. The right of redemption ensures that families can recover their ancestral property.

verses.text: “‘If your brother becomes poor, and sells some of his possessions, then his kinsman who is next to him shall come, and redeem that which his brother has sold.
verses.explainer: If one of your fellow Israelites becomes poor and sells some of their property, their nearest relative is to come and redeem what they have sold. The kinsman-redeemer steps in to keep land in the family.

verses.text: If a man has no one to redeem it, and he becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means to redeem it;
verses.explainer: If there is no one to redeem it but later the person prospers and acquires sufficient means to redeem it.

verses.text: then let him reckon the years since its sale, and restore the surplus to the man to whom he sold it; and he shall return to his property.
verses.explainer: They are to determine the value for the years since they sold it and refund the balance to the one to whom they sold it; they can then go back to their own property.

verses.text: But if he isn’t able to get it back for himself, then what he has sold shall remain in the hand of him who has bought it until the Year of Jubilee: and in the Jubilee it shall be released, and he shall return to his property.
verses.explainer: But if they do not acquire the means to repay, what was sold will remain in the possession of the buyer until the Year of Jubilee. It will be returned in the Jubilee, and they can then go back to their property. The Jubilee is the ultimate safety net -- even if you cannot redeem the land yourself, it comes back automatically at Jubilee.

verses.text: “‘If a man sells a dwelling house in a walled city, then he may redeem it within a whole year after it has been sold. For a full year he shall have the right of redemption.
verses.explainer: Anyone who sells a house in a walled city retains the right of redemption a full year after its sale. City property has different rules than agricultural land.

verses.text: If it isn’t redeemed within the space of a full year, then the house that is in the walled city shall be made sure in perpetuity to him who bought it, throughout his generations. It shall not be released in the Jubilee.
verses.explainer: If it is not redeemed before a full year has passed, the house in the walled city shall belong permanently to the buyer and the buyer's descendants. It is not to be returned in the Jubilee. Urban real estate is treated differently from agricultural land -- it does not revert at Jubilee.

verses.text: But the houses of the villages which have no wall around them shall be accounted for with the fields of the country: they may be redeemed, and they shall be released in the Jubilee.
verses.explainer: But houses in villages without walls around them are to be considered as belonging to the open country. They can be redeemed, and they are to be returned in the Jubilee. Village homes follow agricultural rules.

verses.text: “‘Nevertheless the cities of the Levites, the houses in the cities of their possession, the Levites may redeem at any time.
verses.explainer: The Levites always have the right to redeem their houses in the Levitical towns.

verses.text: The Levites may redeem the house that was sold, and the city of his possession, and it shall be released in the Jubilee; for the houses of the cities of the Levites are their possession among the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: If a Levite does not redeem the house, it reverts at Jubilee because the houses in Levitical towns are their property among the Israelites.

verses.text: But the field of the suburbs of their cities may not be sold; for it is their perpetual possession.
verses.explainer: But the pastureland belonging to their towns must not be sold; it is their permanent possession.

verses.text: “‘If your brother has become poor, and his hand can’t support himself among you; then you shall uphold him. He shall live with you like an alien and a temporary resident.
verses.explainer: If any of your fellow Israelites become poor and are unable to support themselves among you, help them as you would a foreigner and stranger, so they can continue to live among you. Poverty among fellow Israelites triggers an obligation to help.

verses.text: Take no interest from him or profit, but fear your God; that your brother may live among you.
verses.explainer: Do not take interest or any profit from them, but fear your God, so that they may continue to live among you. Charging interest to fellow Israelites in need is forbidden -- profit from poverty is exploitation.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, loans to the poor were emergency relief, not business investments. Charging interest on emergency loans trapped the poor in cycles of debt that could lead to enslavement.
thenVsNow.now: The prohibition on interest (usury) profoundly influenced medieval Christian economics and continues to shape Islamic finance. The broader principle -- that profiting from others' desperation is morally wrong -- challenges modern predatory lending practices.

verses.text: You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit.
verses.explainer: You must not lend them money at interest or sell them food at a profit.

verses.text: I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.
verses.explainer: I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan and to be your God.

verses.text: “‘If your brother has grown poor among you, and sells himself to you; you shall not make him to serve as a slave.
verses.explainer: If any of your fellow Israelites become poor and sell themselves to you, do not make them work as slaves. Israelites may become indentured servants but not chattel slaves -- there is a crucial distinction.

verses.text: As a hired servant, and as a temporary resident, he shall be with you; he shall serve with you until the Year of Jubilee:
verses.explainer: They are to be treated as hired workers or temporary residents among you; they are to work for you until the Year of Jubilee.

verses.text: then he shall go out from you, he and his children with him, and shall return to his own family, and to the possession of his fathers.
verses.explainer: Then they and their children are to be released, and they will go back to their own clans and to the property of their ancestors. Jubilee frees all Israelite indentured servants.

verses.text: For they are my servants, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt. They shall not be sold as slaves.
verses.explainer: Because the Israelites are my servants, whom I brought out of Egypt, they must not be sold as slaves. God's people cannot be permanently enslaved because they already belong to God. God's claim on them supersedes any human claim.

thenVsNow.then: This remarkable statement establishes that all Israelites are God's servants first. No human master can permanently own what belongs to God. This created a ceiling on exploitation within Israel.
thenVsNow.now: The argument that humans belong to God and therefore cannot be owned by other humans became foundational to abolitionist arguments against slavery. Leviticus 25:42 was cited by Christian abolitionists who argued that slavery violated divine ownership of all people.

verses.text: You shall not rule over him with harshness, but shall fear your God.
verses.explainer: Do not rule over them ruthlessly, but fear your God.

verses.text: “‘As for your male and your female slaves, whom you may have; of the nations that are around you, from them you may buy male and female slaves.
verses.explainer: Your male and female slaves are to come from the nations around you; from them you may buy slaves.

verses.text: Moreover of the children of the aliens who live among you, of them you may buy, and of their families who are with you, which they have conceived in your land; and they will be your property.
verses.explainer: You may also buy some of the temporary residents living among you and members of their clans born in your country, and they will become your property.

verses.text: You may make them an inheritance for your children after you, to hold for a possession; of them may you take your slaves forever; but over your brothers the children of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, with harshness.
verses.explainer: You can bequeath them to your children as inherited property and can make them slaves for life, but you must not rule over your fellow Israelites ruthlessly.

verses.text: “‘If an alien or temporary resident with you becomes rich, and your brother beside him has grown poor, and sells himself to the stranger or foreigner living among you, or to a member of the stranger’s family;
verses.explainer: If a foreigner residing among you becomes rich and any of your fellow Israelites become poor and sell themselves to the foreigner or to a member of the foreigner's clan.

verses.text: after he is sold he may be redeemed. One of his brothers may redeem him;
verses.explainer: They retain the right of redemption after they have sold themselves. One of their relatives may redeem them.

verses.text: or his uncle, or his uncle’s son, may redeem him, or any who is a close relative to him of his family may redeem him; or if he has grown rich, he may redeem himself.
verses.explainer: An uncle or a cousin or any blood relative in their clan may redeem them. Or if they prosper, they may redeem themselves.

verses.text: He shall reckon with him who bought him from the year that he sold himself to him to the Year of Jubilee. The price of his sale shall be according to the number of years; he shall be with him according to the time of a hired servant.
verses.explainer: They and their buyer are to count the time from the year they sold themselves up to the Year of Jubilee. The price for their release is to be based on the rate paid to a hired worker for that number of years.

verses.text: If there are yet many years, according to them he shall give back the price of his redemption out of the money that he was bought for.
verses.explainer: If many years remain, they must pay for their redemption a larger share of the price paid for them.

verses.text: If there remain but a few years to the year of jubilee, then he shall reckon with him; according to his years of service he shall give back the price of his redemption.
verses.explainer: If only a few years remain until the Year of Jubilee, they are to compute that and pay for their redemption accordingly.

verses.text: As a servant hired year by year shall he be with him. He shall not rule with harshness over him in your sight.
verses.explainer: They are to be treated as workers hired from year to year; you must see to it that those to whom they owe service do not rule over them ruthlessly.

verses.text: If he isn’t redeemed by these means, then he shall be released in the Year of Jubilee, he, and his children with him.
verses.explainer: Even if someone is not redeemed in any of these ways, they and their children are to be released in the Year of Jubilee.

verses.text: For to me the children of Israel are servants; they are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt. I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: For the Israelites belong to me as servants. They are my servants, whom I brought out of Egypt. I am the Lord your God. The chapter ends where it began: with God's claim on his people. The Jubilee is ultimately about who owns whom -- and the answer is that God owns everyone.

explainer.summary: This climactic chapter presents the consequences of Israel's choice: obedience brings extraordinary blessings (agricultural abundance, peace, victory over enemies, God's presence), while disobedience brings escalating curses (disease, defeat, famine, exile, and devastation). The five stages of increasing punishment show God's patience -- each stage gives Israel another chance to repent before the next level of consequence arrives. But even after the worst curses, God promises he will not utterly destroy Israel or break his covenant with them.
themes: God's patience -- five stages of warning
The unbreakable covenant -- even in exile, God remembers
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 26 concludes the Holiness Code with a covenant renewal ceremony. It follows the pattern of ancient Near Eastern treaty documents: stipulations (the laws) followed by blessings and curses. This chapter is Leviticus's answer to the question: what happens if Israel fails?

verses.text: “‘You shall make for yourselves no idols, and you shall not raise up a carved image or a pillar, and you shall not place any figured stone in your land, to bow down to it; for I am Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not make idols or set up an image or sacred stone or carved stone in your land to bow down before it. I am the Lord your God. The covenant conditions begin with the first commandment: no idols.

verses.text: “‘You shall keep my Sabbaths, and have reverence for my sanctuary. I am Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Observe my Sabbaths and have reverence for my sanctuary. I am the Lord.

verses.text: “‘If you walk in my statutes, and keep my commandments, and do them;
verses.explainer: If you follow my decrees and are careful to obey my commands -- the blessings section begins. 'If' is the operative word.

verses.text: then I will give you your rains in their season, and the land shall yield its increase, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.
verses.explainer: I will send you rain in its season, and the ground will yield its crops and the trees their fruit. Agricultural prosperity -- rain at the right time, abundant harvests.

thenVsNow.then: In an agricultural society wholly dependent on rainfall, the promise of rain 'in its season' was the promise of life itself. Drought meant famine and death.
thenVsNow.now: While modern agriculture is less dependent on rainfall in some regions, the principle that faithful living produces flourishing remains central to many theological traditions. The specific form changes, but the connection between covenant faithfulness and wellbeing persists.

verses.text: Your threshing shall reach to the vintage, and the vintage shall reach to the sowing time. You shall eat your bread to the full, and dwell in your land safely.
verses.explainer: Your threshing will continue until grape harvest and the grape harvest will continue until planting, and you will eat all the food you want and live in safety in your land. Harvests so abundant that they overlap -- you are still processing one when the next begins.

verses.text: “‘I will give peace in the land, and you shall lie down, and no one will make you afraid. I will remove evil animals out of the land, neither shall the sword go through your land.
verses.explainer: I will grant peace in the land, and you will lie down and no one will make you afraid. I will remove wild beasts from the land, and the sword will not pass through your country. Peace, safety, and freedom from fear.

verses.text: You shall chase your enemies, and they shall fall before you by the sword.
verses.explainer: You will pursue your enemies, and they will fall by the sword before you.

verses.text: Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand; and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.
verses.explainer: Five of you will chase a hundred, and a hundred of you will chase ten thousand, and your enemies will fall by the sword before you. Disproportionate military success -- a sign of God fighting on Israel's behalf.

verses.text: “‘I will have respect for you, and make you fruitful, and multiply you, and will establish my covenant with you.
verses.explainer: I will look on you with favor and make you fruitful and increase your numbers, and I will keep my covenant with you.

verses.text: You shall eat old store long kept, and you shall move out the old because of the new.
verses.explainer: You will still be eating last year's harvest when you will have to move it out to make room for the new. Such abundance that you cannot even store it all.

verses.text: I will set my tent among you, and my soul won’t abhor you.
verses.explainer: I will put my dwelling place among you, and I will not abhor you. The ultimate blessing: God's continued presence.

verses.text: I will walk among you, and will be your God, and you will be my people.
verses.explainer: I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be my people. The covenant formula in its purest form. This is what all the laws, sacrifices, and purity regulations exist to protect: the relationship between God and his people.

verses.text: I am Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves. I have broken the bars of your yoke, and made you go upright.
verses.explainer: I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt so that you would no longer be slaves to the Egyptians; I broke the bars of your yoke and enabled you to walk with heads held high. The blessings section ends with the Exodus -- reminding Israel of what God has already done as the basis for trusting what he will do.

verses.text: “‘But if you will not listen to me, and will not do all these commandments;
verses.explainer: But if you will not listen to me and carry out all these commands -- the curses section begins. The 'if' is conditional; Israel has a choice.

verses.text: and if you shall reject my statutes, and if your soul abhors my ordinances, so that you will not do all my commandments, but break my covenant;
verses.explainer: And if you reject my decrees and abhor my laws and fail to carry out all my commands and so violate my covenant --

verses.text: I also will do this to you: I will appoint terror over you, even consumption and fever, that shall consume the eyes, and make the soul to pine away. You will sow your seed in vain, for your enemies will eat it.
verses.explainer: Then I will do this to you: I will bring on you sudden terror, wasting diseases, and fever that will destroy your sight and sap your strength. You will plant seed in vain because your enemies will eat it. First stage: disease and military failure.

verses.text: I will set my face against you, and you will be struck before your enemies. Those who hate you will rule over you; and you will flee when no one pursues you.
verses.explainer: I will set my face against you so that you will be defeated by your enemies. Those who hate you will rule over you, and you will flee even when no one is pursuing you.

verses.text: “‘If you in spite of these things will not listen to me, then I will chastise you seven times more for your sins.
verses.explainer: If after all this you will not listen to me, I will punish you for your sins seven times over. Second escalation: intensified punishment.

verses.text: I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your sky like iron, and your soil like brass.
verses.explainer: I will break down your stubborn pride and make the sky above you like iron and the ground beneath you like bronze. Drought -- the sky produces no rain, and the ground produces no crops.

verses.text: Your strength will be spent in vain; for your land won’t yield its increase, neither will the trees of the land yield their fruit.
verses.explainer: Your strength will be spent in vain, because your soil will not yield its crops, nor will the trees of the land yield their fruit.

verses.text: “‘If you walk contrary to me, and won’t listen to me, then I will bring seven times more plagues on you according to your sins.
verses.explainer: If you remain hostile toward me and refuse to listen to me, I will multiply your afflictions seven times over, as your sins deserve. Third escalation.

verses.text: I will send the wild animals among you, which will rob you of your children, destroy your livestock, and make you few in number. Your roads will become desolate.
verses.explainer: I will send wild animals against you, and they will rob you of your children, destroy your cattle, and make you so few in number that your roads will be deserted.

verses.text: “‘If by these things you won’t be reformed to me, but will walk contrary to me;
verses.explainer: If in spite of these things you do not accept my correction but continue to be hostile toward me.

verses.text: then I will also walk contrary to you; and I will strike you, even I, seven times for your sins.
verses.explainer: I myself will be hostile toward you and will afflict you for your sins seven times over. Fourth escalation: God himself becomes actively hostile.

verses.text: I will bring a sword upon you that will execute the vengeance of the covenant. You will be gathered together within your cities, and I will send the pestilence among you. You will be delivered into the hand of the enemy.
verses.explainer: And I will bring the sword on you to avenge the breaking of the covenant. When you withdraw into your cities, I will send a plague among you.

verses.text: When I break your staff of bread, ten women shall bake your bread in one oven, and they shall deliver your bread again by weight. You shall eat, and not be satisfied.
verses.explainer: When I cut off your supply of bread, ten women will be able to bake your bread in a single oven, and they will dole out the bread by weight. You will eat, but you will not be satisfied. Famine so severe that ten families share one oven, and what they produce is not enough.

verses.text: “‘If you in spite of this won’t listen to me, but walk contrary to me;
verses.explainer: If in spite of this you still do not listen to me but continue to be hostile toward me.

verses.text: then I will walk contrary to you in wrath. I will also chastise you seven times for your sins.
verses.explainer: Then in my anger I will be hostile toward you, and I myself will punish you for your sins seven times over. Fifth and final escalation.

verses.text: You will eat the flesh of your sons, and you will eat the flesh of your daughters.
verses.explainer: You will eat the flesh of your sons and the flesh of your daughters. Cannibalism from famine -- the most horrifying consequence imaginable. This actually occurred during the siege of Jerusalem in 587 BC and again in 70 AD.

verses.text: I will destroy your high places, and cut down your incense altars, and cast your dead bodies upon the bodies of your idols; and my soul will abhor you.
verses.explainer: I will destroy your high places, cut down your incense altars, and pile your dead bodies on the lifeless forms of your idols, and I will abhor you. Complete devastation of both people and places of worship.

verses.text: I will lay your cities waste, and will bring your sanctuaries to desolation. I will not take delight in the sweet fragrance of your offerings.
verses.explainer: I will turn your cities into ruins and lay waste your sanctuaries, and I will take no delight in the pleasing aroma of your offerings.

verses.text: I will bring the land into desolation; and your enemies that dwell therein will be astonished at it.
verses.explainer: I myself will lay waste the land, so that your enemies who settle in it will be appalled.

verses.text: I will scatter you among the nations, and I will draw out the sword after you. Your land will be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.
verses.explainer: I will scatter you among the nations and will draw out my sword and pursue you. Your land will be laid waste, and your cities will lie in ruins. Exile -- the ultimate covenant curse. Removal from the Promised Land is the consequence of persistent unfaithfulness.

verses.text: Then the land will enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate and you are in your enemies’ land. Even then the land will rest and enjoy its Sabbaths.
verses.explainer: Then the land will enjoy its sabbath years all the time that it lies desolate and you are in the country of your enemies; then the land will rest and enjoy its sabbaths. The exile allows the land to recover the sabbath years Israel denied it -- a remarkable ecological theology.

verses.text: As long as it lies desolate it shall have rest, even the rest which it didn’t have in your Sabbaths, when you lived on it.
verses.explainer: All the time that it lies desolate, the land will have the rest it did not have during the sabbaths you lived in it.

verses.text: “‘As for those of you who are left, I will send a faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf will put them to flight; and they shall flee, as one flees from the sword. They will fall when no one pursues.
verses.explainer: As for those of you who are left, I will make their hearts so fearful in the lands of their enemies that the sound of a windblown leaf will put them to flight.

verses.text: They will stumble over one another, as it were before the sword, when no one pursues. You will have no power to stand before your enemies.
verses.explainer: They will stumble over one another as though fleeing from the sword, even though no one is pursuing them. Psychological terror in exile.

verses.text: You will perish among the nations. The land of your enemies will eat you up.
verses.explainer: You will perish among the nations; the land of your enemies will devour you.

verses.text: Those of you who are left will pine away in their iniquity in your enemies’ lands; and also in the iniquities of their fathers they shall pine away with them.
verses.explainer: Those of you who are left will waste away in the lands of their enemies because of their sins; also because of their ancestors' sins they will waste away.

verses.text: “‘If they confess their iniquity, and the iniquity of their fathers, in their trespass which they trespassed against me, and also that, because they walked contrary to me,
verses.explainer: But if they will confess their sins and the sins of their ancestors -- their unfaithfulness and their hostility toward me. Even after the worst curses, the door to repentance remains open. Confession can begin the restoration process.

verses.text: I also walked contrary to them, and brought them into the land of their enemies; if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled, and they then accept the punishment of their iniquity;
verses.explainer: Which made me hostile toward them so that I sent them into the land of their enemies -- then when their uncircumcised hearts are humbled and they pay for their sin.

verses.text: then I will remember my covenant with Jacob; and also my covenant with Isaac, and also my covenant with Abraham; and I will remember the land.
verses.explainer: I will remember my covenant with Jacob and my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land. God remembers the covenant -- even when Israel has broken it beyond recognition. The covenant is stronger than Israel's failure.

thenVsNow.then: This promise of remembrance became the foundation of Jewish hope during the Babylonian exile. Even in the darkest period of national history, God's covenant could not be permanently broken.
thenVsNow.now: The idea that God's commitment to his people survives even catastrophic failure is central to both Jewish and Christian theology. Grace means that the relationship is ultimately secured by God's faithfulness, not human performance.

verses.text: The land also will be left by them, and will enjoy its Sabbaths while it lies desolate without them: and they will accept the punishment of their iniquity; because, even because they rejected my ordinances, and their soul abhorred my statutes.
verses.explainer: For the land will be deserted by them and will enjoy its sabbaths while it lies desolate without them. They will pay for their sins because they rejected my laws and abhorred my decrees.

verses.text: Yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not reject them, neither will I abhor them, to destroy them utterly, and to break my covenant with them; for I am Yahweh their God;
verses.explainer: Yet in spite of this, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not reject them or abhor them so as to destroy them completely, breaking my covenant with them. I am the Lord their God. This is the most important verse in the chapter: even after everything, God will not destroy Israel completely. The covenant survives. This is not unconditional approval but unconditional commitment.

verses.text: but I will for their sake remember the covenant of their ancestors, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God. I am Yahweh.’”
verses.explainer: But for their sake I will remember the covenant with their ancestors whom I brought out of Egypt in the sight of the nations to be their God. I am the Lord. The Exodus covenant is the bedrock -- it cannot be unmade.

verses.text: These are the statutes, ordinances and laws, which Yahweh made between him and the children of Israel in Mount Sinai by Moses.
verses.explainer: These are the decrees, the laws, and the regulations that the Lord established at Mount Sinai between himself and the Israelites through Moses. A closing formula for the Holiness Code and arguably for the main body of Leviticus.

explainer.summary: The final chapter of Leviticus addresses voluntary vows and dedications to God -- the monetary values assigned when people, animals, houses, or land are dedicated to the Lord. It establishes a scale of redemption prices based on age and gender, rules for redeeming dedicated animals and property, and the principle that firstborn animals and tithes already belong to God and cannot be 'dedicated' as if they were voluntary gifts. The chapter serves as an appendix to the main body of laws.
themes: Redemption values for persons and property
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 27 functions as an appendix after the formal conclusion in 26:46. It addresses the practical matter of vows -- what happens when someone makes a spontaneous promise to God. It ensures that even voluntary devotion is orderly and honest.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A tenth
wordStudies.meaning: A tenth. The practice of giving one-tenth of agricultural produce (and later income) to God. In Israel, the tithe was not a voluntary donation but an obligation -- the first ten percent already belonged to God.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Moses, saying,
verses.explainer: The Lord speaks to Moses about the regulations for fulfilling special vows.

verses.text: “Speak to the children of Israel, and say to them, ‘When a man consecrates a person to Yahweh in a vow, according to your valuation,
verses.explainer: If anyone makes a special vow to dedicate a person to the Lord by giving the equivalent value. People could be 'dedicated' to God symbolically, with a monetary equivalent paid instead of actual service.

verses.text: your valuation of a male from twenty years old even to sixty years old shall be fifty shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary.
verses.explainer: Set the value of a male between the ages of twenty and sixty at fifty shekels of silver, according to the sanctuary shekel. The valuation scale begins with the most economically productive group.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Vow valuations', 'explanation': 'These monetary values do not reflect the intrinsic worth of human beings but their economic productivity in an ancient agricultural society. A male in his prime working years could produce the most labor, hence the highest valuation. The scale is about economic capacity, not human dignity.'}

verses.text: If she is a female, then your valuation shall be thirty shekels.
verses.explainer: If it is a female, set her value at thirty shekels. The lower valuation reflects the economic realities of an ancient agrarian society, not a statement about the relative worth of men and women.

verses.text: If the person is from five years old even to twenty years old, then your valuation shall be for a male twenty shekels, and for a female ten shekels.
verses.explainer: If it is a person between the ages of five and twenty, set the value of a male at twenty shekels and of a female at ten shekels.

verses.text: If the person is from a month old even to five years old, then your valuation shall be for a male five shekels of silver, and for a female your valuation shall be three shekels of silver.
verses.explainer: If it is a person between one month and five years, set the value of a male at five shekels of silver and that of a female at three shekels.

verses.text: If the person is from sixty years old and upward; if he is a male, then your valuation shall be fifteen shekels, and for a female ten shekels.
verses.explainer: If it is a person sixty years old or more, set the value of a male at fifteen shekels and of a female at ten shekels.

verses.text: But if he is poorer than your valuation, then he shall be set before the priest, and the priest shall assign a value to him. The priest shall assign a value according to his ability to pay.
verses.explainer: If anyone making the vow is too poor to pay the specified amount, the person being dedicated is to be presented to the priest, who will set the value according to what the vow-maker can afford. Once again, the economic sliding scale appears -- poverty never prevents devotion to God.

verses.text: “‘If it is an animal, of which men offer an offering to Yahweh, all that any man gives of such to Yahweh becomes holy.
verses.explainer: If what they vowed is an animal that is acceptable as an offering to the Lord, such an animal given to the Lord becomes holy.

verses.text: He shall not alter it, nor change it, a good for a bad, or a bad for a good: and if he shall at all change animal for animal, then both it and that for which it is changed shall be holy.
verses.explainer: They must not exchange it or substitute a good one for a bad one, or a bad one for a good one. If they should substitute one animal for another, both it and the substitute become holy. Attempted substitution backfires -- you lose both animals.

verses.text: If it is any unclean animal, of which they do not offer as an offering to Yahweh, then he shall set the animal before the priest;
verses.explainer: If what they vowed is a ceremonially unclean animal -- one that is not acceptable as an offering to the Lord -- the animal must be presented to the priest.

verses.text: and the priest shall value it, whether it is good or bad. As you the priest values it, so shall it be.
verses.explainer: The priest will judge its quality as good or bad. Whatever value the priest then sets, that is what it will be.

verses.text: But if he will indeed redeem it, then he shall add the fifth part of it to its valuation.
verses.explainer: If the owner wishes to redeem the animal, a fifth must be added to its value. The standard twenty percent surcharge for redemption.

verses.text: “‘When a man dedicates his house to be holy to Yahweh, then the priest shall evaluate it, whether it is good or bad: as the priest shall evaluate it, so shall it stand.
verses.explainer: If anyone dedicates their house as something holy to the Lord, the priest will judge its quality as good or bad. Whatever value the priest then sets, so it will remain.

verses.text: If he who dedicates it will redeem his house, then he shall add the fifth part of the money of your valuation to it, and it shall be his.
verses.explainer: If the person who dedicates their house wishes to redeem it, they must add a fifth to its value, and it will again become theirs.

verses.text:
“‘If a man dedicates to Yahweh part of the field of his possession, then your valuation shall be according to the seed for it. The sowing of a homer
 of barley shall be valued at fifty shekels of silver.
verses.explainer: If anyone dedicates to the Lord part of their family land, its value is to be set according to the amount of seed required for it -- fifty shekels of silver for a homer of barley seed.

verses.text: If he dedicates his field from the Year of Jubilee, according to your valuation it shall stand.
verses.explainer: If they dedicate a field during the Year of Jubilee, the value that has been set remains.

verses.text: But if he dedicates his field after the Jubilee, then the priest shall reckon to him the money according to the years that remain to the Year of Jubilee; and an abatement shall be made from your valuation.
verses.explainer: But if they dedicate a field after the Jubilee, the priest will determine the value according to the number of years that remain until the next Year of Jubilee, and its set value will be reduced.

verses.text: If he who dedicated the field will indeed redeem it, then he shall add the fifth part of the money of your valuation to it, and it shall remain his.
verses.explainer: If the person who dedicates the field wishes to redeem it, they must add a fifth to its value, and the field will again become theirs.

verses.text: If he will not redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another man, it shall not be redeemed any more;
verses.explainer: If, however, they do not redeem the field, or if they have sold it to someone else, it can never be redeemed.

verses.text: but the field, when it goes out in the Jubilee, shall be holy to Yahweh, as a devoted field. It shall be owned by the priests.
verses.explainer: When the field is released in the Jubilee, it will become holy, like a field devoted to the Lord; it will become priestly property.

verses.text: “‘If he dedicates a field to Yahweh which he has bought, which is not of the field of his possession,
verses.explainer: If anyone dedicates to the Lord a field they have bought, which is not part of their family land.

verses.text: then the priest shall reckon to him the worth of your valuation up to the Year of Jubilee; and he shall give your valuation on that day, as a holy thing to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The priest will determine its value up to the Year of Jubilee, and the owner must pay its value on that day as something holy to the Lord.

verses.text: In the Year of Jubilee the field shall return to him from whom it was bought, even to him to whom the possession of the land belongs.
verses.explainer: In the Year of Jubilee the field will revert to the person from whom it was bought, the one whose land it was.

verses.text: All your valuations shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary: twenty gerahs to the shekel.
verses.explainer: Every value is to be set according to the sanctuary shekel, twenty gerahs to the shekel. Standardized currency ensures fair valuation.

verses.text: “‘Only the firstborn among animals, which is made Yahweh’s firstborn, no man may dedicate; whether an ox or a sheep. It is Yahweh’s.
verses.explainer: No one may dedicate the firstborn of an animal, since the firstborn already belongs to the Lord; whether an ox or a sheep, it is the Lord's. You cannot 'give' God what already belongs to him. Firstborn animals are already his.

verses.text: If it is an unclean animal, then he shall buy it back according to your valuation, and shall add to it the fifth part of it; or if it isn’t redeemed, then it shall be sold according to your valuation.
verses.explainer: If it is one of the unclean animals, it may be bought back at its set value, plus a fifth. If it is not redeemed, it is to be sold at its set value.

verses.text: “‘Notwithstanding, no devoted thing, that a man shall devote to Yahweh of all that he has, whether of man or animal, or of the field of his possession, shall be sold or redeemed. Everything devoted to destruction is most holy to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: But nothing that a person owns and devotes to the Lord -- whether a human being or an animal or family land -- may be sold or redeemed; everything so devoted is most holy to the Lord. Items under the 'ban' (cherem) -- totally devoted to God -- cannot be reclaimed. This is absolute dedication.

verses.text: “‘No one devoted, who shall be devoted from among men, shall be ransomed; he shall surely be put to death.
verses.explainer: No person devoted to destruction may be ransomed; they are to be put to death. The most extreme form of dedication -- persons under the ban in warfare. This foreshadows the conquest narratives in Joshua.

verses.text: “‘All the tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees, is Yahweh’s. It is holy to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: A tithe of everything from the land, whether grain from the soil or fruit from the trees, belongs to the Lord; it is holy to the Lord. The tithe is not a gift but an acknowledgment of ownership. The first ten percent was always God's.

thenVsNow.then: The tithe supported the Levites, who had no land inheritance, and funded the worship system. It was the primary mechanism for maintaining religious infrastructure.
thenVsNow.now: Tithing remains a practice in many Christian traditions, though its interpretation varies from mandatory obligation to voluntary guideline. The underlying principle -- that our resources ultimately belong to God and a portion should be dedicated to his purposes -- transcends the specific agricultural context.

verses.text: If a man redeems anything of his tithe, he shall add a fifth part to it.
verses.explainer: Whoever would redeem any of their tithe must add a fifth of the value to it. If you want to keep what you owe God, it costs you extra.

verses.text: All the tithe of the herds or the flocks, whatever passes under the rod, the tenth shall be holy to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Every tithe of the herd and flock -- every tenth animal that passes under the shepherd's rod -- will be holy to the Lord. The counting method prevents cherry-picking: you count your animals through, and every tenth one, regardless of quality, belongs to God.

verses.text: He shall not search whether it is good or bad, neither shall he change it. If he changes it at all, then both it and that for which it is changed shall be holy. It shall not be redeemed.’”
verses.explainer: No one may pick out the good from the bad or make any substitution. If anyone does make a substitution, both the animal and its substitute become holy and cannot be redeemed.

verses.text: These are the commandments which Yahweh commanded Moses for the children of Israel on Mount Sinai.
verses.explainer: These are the commands the Lord gave Moses at Mount Sinai for the Israelites. The final verse of Leviticus returns to Sinai. Everything in this book -- every sacrifice, purity law, festival regulation, and ethical command -- flows from God's encounter with Israel at the mountain where heaven and earth met.
